name: HMS Utmost (N 19)
events: Prestwich Urban District adopted HMS Utmost during a fund raising "Warship Week" aimed at raising money to pay for a ship from particular towns. The Town of Prestwich was presented with an oak plaque/shield made of materials from the submarine. The plaque was on display in Prestwich Town Hall until Prestwich joined Bury. The plaque is now in Bury Museum. (1)

The history of HMS Utmost as compiled on this page is extracted from the patrol reports and logbooks of this submarine and was created in June 2013. Corrections and details regarding information from the enemy's side (for instance the composition of convoys attacked) are kindly provided by Mr. Platon Alexiades, a naval researcher from Canada. He also provided details regarding the special operations and train wrecking missions carried out by HMS Utmost.
This page was last updated in November 2025.
15 Aug 1940 Utmost (Lt. J.H. Eaden, DSC, RN) departed her builders yard at Barrow for the Clyde.
[No details available on a possible escort.] (2)
16 Aug 1940 Utmost (Lt. J.H. Eaden, DSC, RN) arrived in the Clyde area for a period of trials and training.
[As no logs for this submarine are available until January 1941 details for the coming months may be missing.] (2)
17 Aug 1940 Having successfully completed her acceptance trials Utmost (Lt. J.H. Eaden, DSC, RN) was taken over from the shipyard and commissioned in full at Rothesay.
10 Sep 1940 HMS Utmost (Lt. J.H. Eaden, DSC, RN) is docked at Ardrossan for a propeller change. (2)
11 Sep 1940 HMS Utmost (Lt. J.H. Eaden, DSC, RN) is undocked. (2)
12 Sep 1940 HMS Otway (Lt. E.D. Norman, RN), HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt. J.H. Eaden, DSC, RN) all conducted exercises in the Clyde area. (3)
14 Sep 1940 HMS Otway (Lt. E.D. Norman, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt. J.H. Eaden, DSC, RN) conducted exercises in the Clyde area. (3)
16 Sep 1940 Around 2300 A/16, HMS Utmost (Lt. J.H. Eaden, DSC, RN) departed Rothesay for Portsmouth.
Passage south through the Irish Sea was made together with HMS Cachalot (Lt.Cdr. J.D. Luce, RN) and HMS Tribune (Lt. E.F. Balston, RN).

The submarines were escorted by HMS Cutty Sark (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H. Mack, RN) until 0540 A/19 when HMS Cachalot and HMS Tribune parted company west of the Scilly Islands. Both submarines proceeded on patrol in the Bay of Biscay.

HMS Utmost then proceeded eastwards escorted by HMS Cutty Sark which arrived at Plymouth later on the 19th. HMS Utmost was then joined by HMS Cape Comorin (T/S.Lt. G.V. Doubleday, RNVR) which escorted her to Portsmouth. (2)
20 Sep 1940 HMS Utmost (Lt. J.H. Eaden, DSC, RN) arrived at Portsmouth (Fort Blockhouse, Haslar Creek, Gosport) from Rothesay. (2)
21 Sep 1940 While bottoming at Haslar Creek during an air raid, HMS Utmost (Lt. J.H. Eaden, DSC, RN) sustained damage to her fore hydroplanes. The damage could be repaired quickly. (4)

25 Sep 1940 Around 1710 A/25, HMS Utmost (Lt. J.H. Eaden, DSC, RN) departed Portsmouth for her 1st war patrol. She was to have sailed on 22 September but was delayed by the damage sustained the day before. She was ordered to patrol in the English Channel (areas S 3 and D 4).
She was escorted out until 2152 A/25. [The identity of the escort is not mentioned in the patrol report.]
[As no log is available for this period no map can be displayed.] (5)
26 Sep 1940 At 0212 A/26, HMS Utmost (Lt. J.H. Eaden, DSC, RN) arrived in the waiting position for area S 3. Commenced patrolling in the vicinity. (5)

27 Sep 1940 HMS Utmost (Lt. J.H. Eaden, DSC, RN) had still not received orders to enter area S 3. However when signals were received to other submarines the conclusion could be drawn that the area was vacant so HMS Utmost proceeded to patrol off the Seine Bay. (5)
1 Oct 1940 HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. J.H. Eaden, DSC, RN) entered area D 4 which is to the east of Le Havre in the Fécamp area. (5)

3 Oct 1940 At 1215 A/3, north of Fécamp, in position 49°52'N, 00°19.5'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. J.H. Eaden, DSC, RN) sighted masts and funnels of a convoy proceeding to the south-west. Range was 9000 yards. The were very close to the coast and most difficult to see against the cliffs. Result was that they were seen too late to be attacked. It appeared the convoy was made up of seven small coasters.

At 1230 A/3, a trawler was sighted bearing 190° at a range of 3 miles. Shortly afterwards this trawler was joined by a second trawler. Both trawlers commenced sweeping towards the submarine so the stern was put towards the enemy. The trawlers closed to 3000 yards and then patrolled that area for 15 minutes before making off to the south-west. The auxiliary patrol vessels VP 1510 and VP 1512 arrived at Fécamp around this time and may have been the vessels sighted. (6)
4 Oct 1940 On surfacing at 2000 A/4, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. J.H. Eaden, DSC, RN) left patrol to return to Portsmouth. (5)

5 Oct 1940 Around 1100 A/5, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. J.H. Eaden, DSC, RN) ended her 1st war patrol at Portsmouth. She, and HMS Swordfish (Lt. P.J. Cowell, RN), were escorted in by HMS Warwick Deeping (Skr. J.R. Bruce, RNR).
There had been problems with 'singing' propellers during the patrol. The propellers were changed twice and several trials were undertaken afterwards. (5)
9 Oct 1940 HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. J.H. Eaden, DSC, RN) is docked for a propeller change. [It is currently not know to us when she was undocked.] (7)
15 Oct 1940 HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. J.H. Eaden, DSC, RN) is docked for another propeller change. [It is currently not know to us when she was undocked.] (7)

28 Oct 1940 In the afternoon, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. J.H. Eaden, DSC, RN) departed Portsmouth for Gibraltar. She was to join the Mediterranean Fleet. En-route she was to make a short 48-hour patrol in the Bay of Biscay making this her 2nd war patrol.
[As no log is available for this period no map can be displayed. Also a patrol / passage report is not available.] (2)

5 Nov 1940 Around 1845 A/5, south-west of Lisbon, in position 37°58'N, 09°50'W, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. J.H. Eaden, DSC, RN) stopped the Portuguese fishing vessel Machado (316 GRT, 1919) but released her after examination.
Timespan: 5 to 23 November 1940.
In response to the attack on convoy HX 84 by the German pocket battleship Admiral Scheer the Admiralty acted quickly.

The battlecruisers HMS Hood (Capt. I.G. Glennie, RN, flying the flag of Vice-Admiral W.J. Whitworth, CB, DSO, RN), HMS Repulse (Capt. W.G. Tennant, CB, MVO, RN), light cruisers HMS Naiad (Capt. M.H.A. Kelsey, DSC, RN, flying the flag of Rear-Admiral E.L.S. King, CB, MVO, RN), HMS Phoebe (Capt. G. Grantham, RN), HMS Bonaventure (Capt. H.J. Egerton, RN) and the destroyers HMS Somali (Capt. C. Caslon, RN), HMS Eskimo (Cdr. St. J.A. Micklethwait, DSO and Bar, RN), HMS Mashona (Cdr. W.H. Selby, RN), HMS Matabele (Cdr. R.St.V. Sherbrooke, DSO, RN), HMS Punjabi (Cdr. J.T. Lean, DSO, RN) and HMS Electra (Lt.Cdr. S.A. Buss, MVO, RN) departed Scapa Flow at 2330 A/5 to proceed to the last reported position of the German pocket battleship 52°50'N, 32°15'W at 2003 A/5.
At 1050 A/6 the force split up; HMS Hood, HMS Naiad, HMS Phoebe, HMS Somali, HMS Eskimo and HMS Punjabi proceeded to patrol off the Bay of Biscay to cover the approaches to Brest and Lorient.
HMS Repulse, HMS Bonaventure, HMS Mashona, HMS Matabele and HMS Electra towards the Admiral Scheer's last known position.

At 0700 A/6 the battleships HMS Nelson (Capt. G.J.A. Miles, RN, flying the flag of Admiral of the Fleet C.M. Forbes, GCB, DSO, RN) and HMS Rodney (Capt. F.H.G. Dalrymple-Hamilton, RN), light cruiser HMS Southampton (Capt. B.C.B. Brooke, RN) and the destroyers HMS Cossack (Capt. P.L. Vian, DSO, RN), HMS Maori (Cdr. H.T. Armstrong, RN), HMS Brilliant (Lt.Cdr. F.C. Brodrick, RN), HMS Douglas (Cdr.(Retd.) J.G. Crossley, RN), HMS Keppel (Lt. R.J. Hanson, RN) and HMS Vimy (Lt.Cdr. D.J.B. Jewitt, RN) departed Scapa Flow to cover the patrols in the Iceland-Faroes Channel.
Shortly before midnight during the night of 6/7 November HMS Rodney was detached to escort to escort convoy HX 83 and once this convoy was safe, HX 85 from Halifax.

Three armed merchant cruisers, which were on patrol were recalled to port on the 8th. These were HMS Chitral (Capt.(Retd.) G. Hamilton, RN), which was to the northwest of Iceland and HMS California (Capt. C.J. Pope, RAN) and HMS Worcestershire (A/Capt. J. Creswell, RN), which were to the south of Iceland. The light cruiser HMS Southampton was ordered to take over the place of HMS Chitral. She split off from HMS Nelson at 1600 A/8. HMS Worcestershire joined HMS Nelson and her escorting destroyers around 1500 A/9.

There were also the destroyers HMS Churchill (Cdr.(Retd.) G.R. Cousins, RN), HMS Lewes (Lt.Cdr. J.N.K. Knight, RN), HMS Lincoln (Cdr. A.M. Sheffield, RN) and HMS Ludlow (Cdr. G.B. Sayer, RN). They were en-route to the U.K. and had departed Halifax on 31 October and refuelled at St. Johns on 3 November. After receiving distress signals from ships in convoy HX 84 they rushed to the reported location. The only thing they found was an empty lifeboat. They then continued their Atlantic crossing and arrived at Londonderry on 9 November.

The destroyer HMS Stanley (A/Lt.Cdr. R.B. Stannard, VC, RNR) had departed Halifax on 1 November and St. Johns on 5 November. Now she and the Canadian destroyer HMCS St.Francis (Lt.Cdr. H.F. Pullen, RCN) escorted convoy HX 85, which had been recalled, back to Nova Scotia.
On 8 November, after machinery defects had been repaired, the heavy cruiser HMAS Australia (Capt. R.R. Stewart, RN) departed the Clyde to protect convoys.

The battlecruiser HMS Renown (Capt C.E.B. Simeon, RN) and the destroyers HMS Encounter (Lt.Cdr. E.V.St J. Morgan, RN), HMS Forester (Lt.Cdr. E.B. Tancock, RN) and HMS Gallant (Lt.Cdr. C.P.F. Brown, RN) departed Gibraltar at 0500 A/6 to provide cover for convoys HG 46 and SL 53.

At 1225 A/6, off Cape St Vincent, the submarine HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. J.H. Eaden, DSC, RN), on passage from Portsmouth to Gibrtaltar, was identified as enemy by HMS Encounter which then rammed the submarine. HMS Encounter was escorted to Gibraltar by HMS Forester. They arrived at 0800 A/7.

On 11 November, HMAS Australia relieved Renown from covering convoy HG 46 and Renown arrived back at Gibraltar around 1515 A/12. Renown had been joined at 0807 A/12 by the destroyers HMS Duncan (Cdr. A.D.B. James, RN) and HMS Forester.

Aircraft carrier HMS Argus (Capt. E.G.N. Rushbrooke, DSC, RN), light cruiser HMS Despatch (Commodore 2nd cl. C.E. Douglas-Pennant, DSC, RN) and the destroyers HMS Verity (Cdr. R.H. Mills, RN), HMS Vesper (Lt.Cdr. W.F.E. Hussey, DSC, RN) and HMS Windsor (Lt.Cdr. G.P. Huddart, RN) departed the Clyde on 7 November for Gibraltar and were also ordered to keep a look out for the German pocket battleship. The destroyers were later detached; HMS Windsor around 0100 A/9 and HMS Verity and HMS Vesper around 0600 A/9. HMS Despatch was detached at 1000 A/13 and proceeded to Gibraltar where she arrived around noon the next day. Shortly before HMS Despatch was detached the destroyers HMS Wishart (Cdr. E.T. Cooper, RN) and HMS Wrestler (Lt. E.L. Jones, DSC, RN) had joined followed later in the day by HMS Vidette (Lt. E.N. Walmsley, RN). HMS Argus, HMS Vidette, HMS Wishart and HMS Wrestler arrived at Gibraltar very late on the 14th.
Battlecruiser HMS Repulse escorted by the destroyers HMS Matabele and HMS Electra arrived at Scapa Flow for refuelling around 1100 A/11.
Light cruiser HMS Bonaventure and destroyer HMS Mashona arrived at Scapa Flow around 1130 A/11 for refuelling.

Battlecruiser HMS Hood, light cruisers HMS Naiad, HMS Phoebe and the destroyers HMS Somali, HMS Eskimo and HMS Punjabi returned to Scapa Flow around 1400 A/11 for refuelling. HMS Eskimo had suffered weather damage to her asdic dome and had some forecastle deck plates buckled. She was docked for repairs in the floating drydock at Scapa Flow from 13 to 16 November. HMS Phoebe had also sustained weather damage for which she was later taken in hand for repairs at Glasgow.

After fuelling HMS Bonaventure departed Scapa Flow at 2300 A/11 to continue to search for survivors from convoy HX 84. Armed merchant cruiser HMS Chitral was also back at sea to search for survivors. She had departed from Reykjavik, Iceland around 2330 A/10.
HMS Bonaventure returned to Scapa Flow on the 19th with weather damage.
The armed merchant cruiser HMS Letitia (A/Capt. E.H. Longsdon, RN) departed the Clyde around 1300 A/11 for the Northern Patrol.

HMS Repulse, HMS Naiad departed Scapa Flow around 1330 A/12 for patrol and also to provide cover for ships of the Northern Patrol. They were escorted by the destoyers HMS Sikh (Cdr. G.H. Stokes, RN), HMS Mashona, HMS Matabele and HMS Punjabi.
HMS Naiad parted company on the 13th to proceed to Jan Mayen Island where a German weather / wireless station in Jameson Bay was to be raided.

HMS Repulse returned to Scapa Flow at 0015 A/19 being escorted by the destroyers HMS Ashanti (Cdr. W.G. Davis, RN), HMS Mashona and HMS Matabele. They had provided cover for HMS Naiad during her raid on Jan Mayen Island.
The battleship HMS Nelson arrived at Scapa Flow around 1630 A/13 escorted by the destroyers Maori, HMS Beagle (Lt.Cdr. R.H. Wright, RN), HMS Bulldog (Lt.Cdr. F.J.G. Hewitt, RN) and Keppel.
Battleship HMS Rodney only arrived at Scapa Flow around 1500 A/23. She had been joined at dawn the previous day by the destroyers HMS Beagle, HMS Brilliant, HMS Bulldog and HMS Electra. (8)

6 Nov 1940 The battlecruiser HMS Renown (Capt C.E.B. Simeon, RN) and the destroyers HMS Encounter (Lt.Cdr. E.V.St J. Morgan, RN), HMS Forester (Lt.Cdr. E.B. Tancock, RN) and HMS Gallant (Lt.Cdr. C.P.F. Brown, RN) departed Gibraltar at 0500 A/6 to provide cover for convoys HG 46 and SL 53.

At 1225 A/6, off Cape St Vincent, the submarine HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. J.H. Eaden, DSC, RN) was identified as enemy by HMS Encounter which then rammed the submarine which was en-route to Gibraltar. HMS Encounter was escorted to Gibraltar by HMS Forester. They arrived at 0800 A/7.

On 11 November, HMAS Australia relieved Renown from covering convoy HG 46 and Renown arrived back at Gibraltar around 1515/12. Renown had been joined at 0807/12 by the destroyers HMS Duncan (Cdr. A.D.B. James, RN) and HMS Forester. (9)

7 Nov 1940 HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. J.H. Eaden, DSC, RN) arrived at Gibraltar where repairs were undertaken to make her seaworthy again and made her able to dive. Permanent repairs were to be undertaken at Malta. There was damage to the casing but also some damage to the pressure hull. (2)
30 Nov 1940 HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. J.H. Eaden, DSC, RN) departed Gibraltar for Malta.
[As no log and passage report is available for this period no map can be displayed.] (2)

8 Dec 1940 HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. J.H. Eaden, DSC, RN) arrived at Malta from Gibraltar. Before she was to be used operationally she had to undergo further repairs to the damage sustained when she was rammed by HMS Encounter (Lt.Cdr. E.V.St J. Morgan, RN) on 6 November. (2)
30 Jan 1941 HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) is undocked from No.1 graving dock at the Malta Dockyard.
[It is not known to us when she was docked.] (10)
1 Feb 1941 HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) conducted exercises off Malta with HMS Decoy (Cdr. E.G. McGregor, DSO, RN). (11)

4 Feb 1941 HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) departed Malta for her 3rd war patrol (1st in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol the western approaches to Tripoli, Libya in area 'Y'. south of 33°30'N, between 11°30'E and 12°20'E.
On departure exercises were carried out with HMS Fermoy (Lt.Cdr. J.G.D. Wetherfield, RN) and HMS Jade (?). (12)
4 Feb 1941 For the daily and attack positions of Utmost during her 3rd war patrol see the map below.
6 Feb 1941 At 0900 A/9, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) entered her patrol area off Tripoli, area 'Y'. (5)

9 Feb 1941 HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) made a torpedo attack on an enemy convoy about 5 nautical miles north-east of Zuwara, Libya in position 33°00'N, 12°11'E. Three torpedoes were fired but no hits were obtained.

The convoy was made up of the transport Nirvo (Italian, 5164 GRT, built 1919) and the tankers Berbera (Italian, 2093 GRT, built 1931) and Caucaso (Italian, 2065 GRT, built 1920) screened by the Italian armed merchant cruiser Attilio Deffenu (3510 GRT, built 1929). The convoy sailed from Naples at 0910-1010 B/6 and proceeded at an average speed of 6.4 knots for Tripoli where it arrived at 1210 B/9. The convoy had stopped to allow another convoy to exit from Tripoli (comprising the Italian troop transports / liners Marco Polo (12567 GRT, built 1912), Conte Rosso (17897 GRT, built 1922) and Esperia (11398 GRT, built 1920) and the motorship Calitea escorted by the Italian destroyers Luca Tarigo, Lanzerotto Malocello, Frecci and Saetta, which sailed from Tripoli at 1830 B/9). The attack was unobserved.

The ships seen earlier, at 0615 A/9, were not two merchant vessels escorted by two destroyers but four Italian destroyers, Luca Tarigo, Lanzerotto Malocello, Freccia and Saetta which sailed from Palermo at 0920-1140 B/8 and arrived at Tripoli at 1030-1120 B/9. The destroyers were required for escort duty for a return convoy of troopships from Tripoli to Naples. No merchant ships arrived at Tripoli on 9 February.
0610 A/9 - In position 32°59'N, 12°13'E, picked up HE bearing 180°.
0615 A/9 - Sighted two merchant ships escorted by two destroyers bearing 180° at a range of 6 miles. Enemy course was 100° at 13 knots.
Utmost turned towards and prepared to fire three torpedoes but owing to the long range it was decided not to give the submarines position away with the very slight chance of a hit.
1715 A/9 - In position 33°00'N, 12°11'E sighted a convoy of three merchant vessels bearing 280°. Range was about 5 nautical miles. They were zig-zagging very erratically. Started attack.
1745 A/9 - The convoy stopped to wait for a fourth merchant vessel that came from the north.
1805 A/9 - The convoy proceeded. It was now getting dark.

1815 A/9 - When about to open fire on the leading ship from 500 yards she suddenly altered course towards. Went deep with the intention to return to periscope depth at the other side of the convoy and then to attack the rear ship. Three torpedoes were fired in this attack but due to the bad visibility no hits were obtained. No counter attack followed. (5)
12 Feb 1941 HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) torpedoed and damaged the transport Mauly (Italian, 5942 GRT, built 1925) off Zuwara, Libya in position 33°00'N, 12°00'E.

The convoy attacked was made up of the above mentioned Mauly as well as the transports Tembien (Italian, 5584 GRT, built 1914) and Leverkusen (German, 7382 GRT, built 1928). They were escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Centauro. Mauly was hit in the stern and immobilised, her crew had abandoned ship but later reboarded her. The Italian auxiliary Orlando and the Italian tug Polifemo were sent from Tripoli to take her in tow and the torpedo boat Rosolino Pilo relieved Centauro which continued with the two remaining ships. It took seven days to bring the damaged Mauly back to Tripoli.
This convoy had sailed from Tripoli at 0405 B/12 and arrived at Naples (minus the Mauly) at 0950 B/14.

0930 A/12 - Sighted a convoy of three transports escorted by one destroyer bearing 100°, range 3 nautical miles, enemy course 260°. Started attack on the leading and largest ship of about 8000 tons. Fired three torpedoes and obtained one hit. Utmost went to 80 feet after firing the torpedoes.
0954 A/12 to 1022 A/12 - 25 Depth charges were dropped. HE indicated that two escorts were hunting. No damage was sustained. Utmost retired to the north-east.
1130 A/12 - Returned to periscope depth. Saw the target down by the stern and stopped. Aircraft were patrolling the area. The convoy meanwhile made off to the west. (5)

13 Feb 1941 At 1355 A/13, north of Zuara, Libya, in approximate position 33°19'N, 12°06'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) sighted a large merchant ship escorted by four destroyers bearing 201° at a range of 8 miles. Enemy course was 100° at approximately 12 knots. Altered course to 270° in case the target should alter to the north. She however hugged the coast bound for Tripoli.
This sighting is very odd as no ships arrived at Tripoli on 13 February.
At 2000 A/13, HMS Utmost left patrol to return to Malta. (5)
15 Feb 1941 Around 0745 A/15, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) ended her 3rd war patrol (1st in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (5)
25 Feb 1941 Around 1800 A/25, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) departed Malta for her 4th war patrol (2nd in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to perform a special operation.
For the daily positions of HMS Utmost during this patrol see the map below.

28 Feb 1941 At 1900 A/28, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) surfaced in position 35°59'N, 10°34'E (Gulf of Hammamet) and carried out the special mission. This was the first of five missions that Utmost would undertake in Tunisia. Although no official report appears to have survived, there is little doubt that HMS Utmost made contact with a sailing / fishing vessel at 1930 A/28 and transferred stores for the Mounier network. The previous month, Mounier had sailed from Tunisia in the sailing vessel Pescadou and reached Malta. He offered his services to organize a resistance network in Tunisia. They were later provided with limpets to sabotage Italian vessels engaged in the phosphate traffic. (5)

1 Mar 1941 A few hours later the submarine sent a boat to pick up 2nd Lt. Fairclough near Sebkhet Halk El Menzel (souh of Hergla). This officer has not been definitely identified. Was he an agent or perhaps a flyer shot down over Tunisia? Any help in identifying him is appreciated.
The special operation was completed at 0520 A/1. HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) then set course to patrol off the Gulf of Hammamet near position 'X' (36°00'N, 11°10'E).
At 1700 A/1, HMS Utmost arrived in position two miles to the south-west of position 'X' and commenced offensive patrol.

2 Mar 1941 At 1030 B/1, in approximate position 36°00'N, 11°10'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) heard distant HE (turbines) bearing 290°. Nothing was seen at periscope depth. Six distant depth charges were heard at 5 minute intervals. The HE faded to the southward.
At 1320 B/2, six more depth charges were heard at 5 minute intervals.

At 1600 B/2, in position 36°02'N, 11°10'E, three merchant ships were sighted escorted by aircraft on bearing 245° at a range of 7 miles. Enemy course was 170°. The distance off track was too great to attack.

This was the convoy made up of the Italian transport Amsterdam (10873 GRT, built 1921) and the German transports Ruhr (5955 GRT, built 1926), Castellon (2068 GRT, built 1935) and Maritza (2910 GRT, built 1936) escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Clio, Pegaso and Orione. They had sailed from Naples at 0200 B/1 and arrived at Tripoli at 1800 B/3. At 1620 B/2, in position 35°59'N, 11°03'E, an escorting aircraft manoeuvred as if it had sighted a submarine, Pegaso dropped one depth charge for intimidation purposes and another one in position 35°54'N, 11°04'E, both were set for a depth of 25 metres.

At 1705 B/2, in position 36°00'N, 11.10'E, sighted a merchant vessel escorted by aircraft on bearing 250° at a range of 4.5 miles. Enemy course was 135°. Closed at high speed for 15 minutes but broke off the attack when the ship altered course to the southward to round Kuriat Island. A second ship was sighted on approximately the same bearing at this time. Both ships were most likely Vichy-French.
At 2015 B/2, HMS Utmost surfaced and left patrol to return to Malta. (5)
4 Mar 1941 Around 0815 B/4, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) ended her 4th war patrol (2nd in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (5)
6 Mar 1941 Around 1200 B/6, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) departed Malta for her 5th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the Gulf of Hammamet.
On departure attack exercises were carried out during which HMS Jade (?) acted as target. (5)
6 Mar 1941 For the daily and attack positions of HMS Utmost during her 5th war patrol see the map below.

7 Mar 1941 In a signal timed 1921 B/7, the Cdr. S.1, informed HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) of the following; Information supports opinion that enemy minelaying took place on Cape Bon - Tripoli route on 24/2. Do not approach within 15 miles of Kelibia or Kuriat. (13)

9 Mar 1941 HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian transport Capo Vita (5683 GRT, built 1916) off the Gulf of Hammamet about 30 nautical miles north-east of Sousse, Tunisia in position 36°09'N, 11°07'E.

At 1600 B/6, this convoy, made up of the above mentioned Capo Vita as well as the Italian transports Fenicia (2584 GRT, built 1919), Caffaro (6476 GRT, built 1924) and the naval water tanker Tanaro (1339 tons, built 1940), escorted by the armed merchant cruiser Attilio Deffenu (3510 GRT, built 1929) and torpedo boat Generale Achille Papa, sailed from Naples for Tripoli. At 1805 B/7, the convoy received from Supermarina the order to proceed to Trapani where the vessels anchored at 2230 B/7. At 1830 B/8, Capo Vita and Fenicia, escorted by Attilio Deffenu and Generale Achille Papa, sailed from Trapani. When off Favignana, the torpedo boat received the order to return to Trapani. The three other ships proceeded. When Capo Vita blew up with an enormous explosion, covering the other two ships with debris, Deffenu fired a single round in the presumed direction of the submarine and then dropped five depth charges set at 50 metres and then looked for survivors but none were found (one man on Fenicia was also missing). At 0655 B/10, Fenicia was torpedoed by HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN). Deffenu searched for the submarine and dropped depth charges, but found nothing then proceeded to recover the survivors. The destroyer Baleno arrived on the scene to help. Fenicia finally sank at 0900 hours. The torpedo boats Clio and Centauro had sailed at 1900 B/9 from Tripoli to assist and arrived on the scene of the sinking of Fenicia at 0935 B/10 but did not participate in the search for survivors and were ordered by Baleno to proceed to join a nearby convoy escorted by the destroyers Fulmine and Turbine. At 1249 B/10, the two torpedo boats interrupted their escort duty as they were ordered by Marilibia to hunt the submarine which had sunk Fenicia. They returned to the area without finding anything. In the meantime, Deffenu arrived in Tripoli at 1656 B/10.
1115 B/9 - Sighted two merchant ships escorted by an armed merchant cruiser bearing 345°, range 5 nautical miles, enemy course 170°. Started attack.

1205 B/9 - Fired three torpedoes from 1000 yards. Two explosions followed. A counter attack followed in which six depth charges were dropped but these did no damage. Twelve minutes later Utmost returned to periscope depth to find the armed merchant cruiser and one of the merchants in sight. The other one must have sunk. She had been heavily laden and was of about 8000 tons and it was also observed that there were troops on board. (5)

10 Mar 1941 At 0730 B/10, in approximate position 36°00'N, 11°15'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) sighted a destroyer bearing 340° at a range of 6 miles. She appeared to be searching near the position of yesterday's attack.

At 1630 B/10, a destroyer was sighted bearing 210° at a range of 6 miles. Enemy course was 330°. Eleven depth charges were heard during the afternoon. A total of 34 depth charges had been counted since yesterday's attack.

The Italian torpedo boats Sagittario and Alcione sailed from Trapani at 1650-1705 B/9 to hunt the submarine which had sunk the Italian transport Capo Vita (see 9 March 1941) but sighted nothing. And at 0813 B/11 they left the area for Porto Empedocle where they arrived at 1608-1625 B/11.
At 2015 B/10, HMS Utmost surfaced and left patrol to return to Malta.

At 2214 B/10, the Cdr. S.1's signal timed 2003 B/10 was received which stated that two enemy merchant ships that were unescorted passed Kelibia at 1700 B/10 southwards, speed 8 knots. HMS Utmost was too far east to intercept. We could not find unescorted Italian ships at that time. Most likely these ships were Vichy French. (5)
12 Mar 1941 Around 0730 B/12, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) ended her 5th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (5)

19 Mar 1941 Around 1700 B/19, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) departed Malta for her 6th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off Kerkennah as a convoy believed to be transporting German troops was expected to sail from Naples bound for Tripoli.

This referred to the convoy made up of the German transports Arcturus (2576 GRT, built 1937), Procida (1842 GRT, built 1927), Santa Fe (4627 GRT, built 1921) and Wachtfels (8467 GRT, built 1928) escorted by the Italian destroyers Saetta, Fulmine and Baleno. The convoy sailed from Naples at 0050-0130 B/19 and arrived at Tripoli at 1327-1535 B/21.
For the daily and attack positions of HMS Utmost during this patrol see the map below.

20 Mar 1941 In a signal timed 1403 B/20, the Cdr. S.1 informed HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) that a convoy of six merchant vessels escorted by four destroyers had passed Kelibia Roads Light southwards at 0945 B/20.

This referred to the convoy made up of the German transports Arcturus (2576 GRT, built 1937), Procida (1842 GRT, built 1927), Santa Fe (4627 GRT, built 1921) and Wachtfels (8467 GRT, built 1928) escorted by the Italian destroyers Saetta, Fulmine and Baleno on passage from Naples (departed 0050-0130 B/19) to Tripoli (arrived 1327-1535 B/21).
In a signal timed 1153 C/20, the Capt. S.1, ordered HMS Ursula and HMS Utmost that if the convoy is escorted by a cruiser the primary object is to attack the transports.

In a signal timed 1811 B/20, the Vice-Admiral Malta, informed HMS Ursula and HMS Utmost of the results of the air reconnaissance on this day in the Sicilian Channel. The following had been reported (All times GMT) (A) One M.V. 5000 ton in position 36°20'N, 12° 20'E, course 270° at 1046 hours (B) One large M.V. in position 37°10'N, 11°40'E, course 180° at 1208 hours (C) Four destroyers and five 5 large M.V’s, 20 miles north-west of Pantellaria, course 350° with flying boat escort at 1215 hours
At 1845 B/20, smoke was sighted on the horizon bearing 206° at an estimated range of 10 miles. This was probably the convoy referred to in the Cdr. S.1's signal timed 1403 B/20.

At 2300 B/20, HMS Utmost proceeded to patrol in an area bounded by 35°00'N to 35°40'N and 12°10'E to 13°00'E in accordance with the Cdr. S.1's signal timed 1441 B/20. In this signal she was also ordered not to enter this area before 0600 B/21. (13)
21 Mar 1941 At 0820 B/21, south-west of Lampedusa, while in approximate position 35°22'N, 12°23'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) sighted smoke bearing 030°.
At 0955 B/21, masts were sighted bearing 355° at a range of about 6 miles. This were thought to be torpedo boats operating from Lampedusa which were again sighted the following day.
[No torpedo boats were operating from Lampedusa at this time though.] (5)

22 Mar 1941 At 0830 B/22, west-south-west of Lampedusa, while in approximate position 35°25'N, 12°19'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) sighted smoke and masts of two trawlers and two torpedo boats bearing 140° at a range of about 5 miles. These vessels had come out of Lampedusa harbour and the seemed to be either hunting, sweeping or exercising in position 35°27'N, 12°25'E. No depth charges were heard but flag hoists were observed.
At 1600 B/22, smoke and masts disappeared towards Lampedusa harbour. One vessel appeared to pass to the eastward of the Island and was observed to the northward.

24 Mar 1941 At 0900 B/24, north-west of Lampedusa, in position 35°44'N, 12°17'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) sighted a small steam vessel of 1000 tons and three auxiliary schooners bearing 003° at a range of 5 miles. Enemy course was 120°.

At 1005 B/24, the steam vessel altered course to the southward to pass between Lampedusa and Lampione Islands. The schooners turned to the eastward when south of Lampedusa and then entered the harbour.

The ships sighted were the steamer Regulus (Italian 1085 GRT, built 1884), the tug Nettuno (Italian, 406 GRT, built 1921) and the Italian schooners Romagna (195 GRT, built 1940), Vanna (279 GRT, built 1918) and Carolina (227 GRT, built 1891) sailed from Trapani at 2000 B/21. They arrived at Lampedusa on 24 March [We do not have the time, but it was after 1005 B/24 according to the patrol report of HMS Utmost]. They sailed from Lampedusa at 0835 B/25 for Zuara (apparently except Regulus which went on to Tripoli, [We do not have the arrival time at Zuara]. Regulus arrived at Tripoli at 1650 B/27, the other four ships arrived at Tripoli at 1010 B/30. (5)

27 Mar 1941 In a signal timed 2350 B/27, the Cdr. S.1 informed HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) that a convoy of six ships with German military units on board was expected to leave Salerno on 27 or 28 March. HMS Utmost was to proceed with despatch to Area 'K' (35°20'N, 11°10'E - 000° - 30 - 281(?).

This signal referred to the convoy made up of the transports Adana (German, 4176 GRT, built 1922), Galilea (Italian, 8040 GRT, built 1916), Heraklea (German, 1927 GRT, built 1922), Ruhr (German, 5954 GRT, built 1926) and Samos (German, 2576 GRT, built 1923) escorted by the Italian destroyers Folgore, Dardo and Strale. It sailed from Naples at 2030-2345 B/26. At 2203 B/28, Heraklea and Ruhr were hit by torpedoes, the former sank, the latter was taken in tow by Dardo and arrived at Trapani at 1845 B/30. The torpedo boats Polluce and Partenope sailed from Tripoli at 0940 B/29 to reinforce the escort. The remaining ships arrived at Tripoli at 0700-0800 B/30. (14)

28 Mar 1941 HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) attacked a convoy of five German transports and three Italian destroyers and torpedoed and sank the Heraklea (1927 GRT, built 1922) and torpedoed and damaged the Ruhr (5954 GRT, built 1926) 22 nautical miles south-east of Kuriat, Tunisia in position 35°40'N, 11°19'E.

The convoy attacked was made up of the two above mentioned transports as well as the German transports Adana (4176 GRT, built 1922) and Samos (2576 GRT, built 1923) and the Italian transport Galilea (8040 GRT, built 1916). They were escorted by the Italian destroyers Folgore, Dardo and Strale. The convoy was on passage from Naples to Tripoli. Heraklea was carrying 212 German soldiers and 100 vehicles, 78 were lost. Ruhr carried 585 German soldiers and 160 vehicles, she was towed by the destroyer Dardo to Trapani where she arrived at 1845 B/30.
The remaining ships of the convoy arrived at Tripoli at 0700-0800 B/30.

2135 B/28 - In position 35°40'N, 11°19'E sighted six heavily laden merchant vessels escorted by two destroyers, thought to be Gregale-class, bearing 330°, range 9000 yards, enemy course 150°, speed 12 knots. Started attack.

The convoy was in two columns and well bunched up except for two laggards, one of which was belching clouds of smoke in her endeavours to maintain 12 knots. One destroyer was zigzagging ahead of the convoy and the other appeared to be whipping up the laggards.

2158 B/28 - Fired four torpedoes at three ships showing up as an unbroken line against the horizon. After the last torpedo had left the tubes Utmost dived. Two hits were obtained. The time between the explosions indicated that in either column a ship must have been hit. No counter attack followed which might indicate that there were survivors in the water. Utmost retired to the eastward and remained dived until 2400 hours. (5)

30 Mar 1941 Around 2200 B/30, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) left patrol to return to Malta in accordance with the Cdr. S.1's signal timed 1125 B/28. HMS Utmost was to return through positions 35°20'N, 13°00'E and 35°37'N, 14°07'E. (14)
1 Apr 1941 Around 0730 B/1, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) ended her 6th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (5)
2 Apr 1941 HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) is docked in No.1 graving dock at the Malta Dockyard. (15)
7 Apr 1941 HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) is undocked. (15)
17 Apr 1941 Around 1830 B/17, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) departed Malta for her 7th war patrol (5th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to perform a special operation.
On departure exercises were to be carried out with HMS Coral (?) but these were cancelled. (5)
17 Apr 1941 For the daily positions of HMS Utmost during her 7th war patrol see the map below.

At 1110 B/19, in position 36°01'N, 10°40'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) sighted a laden merchant ship of about 5000 tons bearing 210° at a range of 4 miles. Enemy course was 340°. An attack was started but later broken off when it was realised the ship was within French territorial waters and that she therefore may be Vichy-French. It was later found out it was an Italian sulphate ship.

The was most probably the Italian transport Capacitas (5371 GRT, built 1910), carrying phosphate, which sailed from Sousse for Palermo on 19 April 1941. Capacitas had just participated in the rescue of the survivors from the Tarigo convoy and landed 138 of them at Sousse.
At 1240 B/19, in position 36°00'N, 10°39'E, a laden 3000 tons merchant vessel was sighted bearing 330° at a range of 5 miles. This was a Vichy-French vessel proceeding on course 160°.

At 2145 B/19, in position 35°57'N, 10°36'E (north of Sousse) HMS Utmost surfaced to carry out the special operation. The Breuillac/Mounier resistance network (see 28 February) had received a signal at noon the previous day that a submarine would pick up Breuillac at 2200 B/19. In Malta, he was to meet with Lt. Col. Des Essarts, an envoy of General De Gaulle, who was to fly from Cairo. Breuillac, who was Chief of Staff to General Jurion, had secured a 15-day leave to do some 'tourism' in Tunisia. He lost no time and travelled by train to Sousse with Mounier and another member of their network, Verdier. They took a small sailboat to go 'fishing' and cruised for several hours at the rendezvous point. Utmost delivered stores and Mounier and Verdier returned with them leaving Breuillac to continue on to Malta.
The operation was completed around 0400 B/20 but not before a southbound convoy passed in close proximity (see 20 April 1941).

20 Apr 1941 At 0315 B/20, in position 35°57'N, 10°33'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN), not having completed the special operation yet (see 19 April), sighted a convoy of four transports and three destroyers bearing 060° at a range of 3 miles. Enemy course was 160°. The convoy passed within a mile and the escorts within half a mile of the submarine who could not be seen owing to her proximity of the land. Owing to her position and the nature of her occupation (the special operation) nothing could be done.

The convoy sighted must have been the one made up of the Italian transports Isarco (5738 GRT, built 1924), Maddalena Odero (5545 GRT, built 1921), Nicolò Odero (6003 GRT, built 1925) and the Italian tanker Alberto Fassio (2289 GRT, built 1914) . They were escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Giuseppe La Farina, Antonio Mosto and Climene. The convoy, except the Alberto Fassio and Climene sailed from Palermo at 2245-2255 B/18. At 0825 B/19, the convoy had been joined by the Alberto Fassio and Climene which had sailed from Trapani at 0445 B/19. The convoy arrived at Tripoli at 1700-1900 B/21.

At 0400 B/20, the special operation was completed. During the special operation information was received that there was an Italian sulphate ship at anchor outside the harbour of Sousse. Course was set to close the harbour to attack it. However the ship was not sighted and course was set to pass close to Monastir and Kuriat but nothing was sighted there either.
At 2100 B/20, course was set to return to Malta. (5)
22 Apr 1941 Around 0655 B/22, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) ended her 7th war patrol (5th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She went to sea again from 1010 B/22 to 2025 B/22, most likely to evade enemy air attacks. (5)
25 Apr 1941 Around 2000 B/25, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) departed Malta for her 8th war patrol (6th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to carry out a special operation.

27 Apr 1941 At 2105 B/27, in position 35°58'N, 10°37.5'E HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) surfaced to carry out the special operation. This was to meet a sailboat manned by Mounier and Verdier and transfer Commandant Breuillac. The submarine cruised in a vain without meeting them, the two men later affirming that they had been at the rendezvous. (5)

28 Apr 1941 At 2106 B/28, in position 35°58'N, 10°37'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) surfaced again to carry out the special operation. This time the sailboat was sighted at 2140 hours and Breuillac and stores (including limpets for underwater sabotage) were transferred ashore and the operation was completed. He had three days to spare for his furlough!
On completion of the opertion HMS Utmost set course for Kuriat Island. (5)
29 Apr 1941 At 0200 B/29, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) commenced patrol in area 'H' between Latitudes 35°50'N and 36°40'N and Longitudes 10°32'E and 11°22'E. (5)
2 May 1941 At 0600 B/2, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) proceeded to patrol in area 'J' (35°10'N, 10°40'E - 000° - 50 - 40) in accordance with Cdr. S.1's signals timed 1251 B/1 and 2003 B/1. (5)

6 May 1941 At 1500 B/15, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) left patrol to return to Malta in accordance with the Cdr. S.1's signal timed 1215 B/5. She is to proceed through position 35°40'N, 13°03'E to arrive off Malta at 0600 B/8. (16)

8 May 1941 At 0430 B/8, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) sighted Filfla Island bearing 340° at a range of 3 miles. HMS Utmost then remained in the Submarine Sanctuary in accordance with the Cdr. S.1's signal timed 1315 B/7.
Due to the amount of traffic in the area for a convoy operation HMS Utmost was not allowed to enter harbour on this day. (5)
9 May 1941 For the daily and attack positions of HMS Utmost during her 9th war patrol see the map below.
9 May 1941 At 0800 B/9 HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) proceeded up the searched channel to enter harbour.
At 0802 B/9, five destroyers were seen bearing 060°.
At 0825 B/9, HMS Utmost was challenged by HMS Hotspur (Lt.Cdr. C.P.F. Brown, DSC, RN) which was one of the five destroyers.
At 0940 B/9, four aircraft were seen bearing 350° at a height of 1000 feet. Crash dived. One minute later 16 bombs were dropped causing some minor damage.
At 1019 B/9, HMS Utmost surfaced.
At 1025 B/9, an aircraft seen on course 090° at a height of 500 feet. Again crash dived.
At 1055 B/9, HMS Utmost surfaced.
At 1150 B/9, HMS Utmost finally arrived off Marsamxett harbour. She was however still not allowed to enter the harbour due to mines.
After HMS Swona (T/Lt. A.C.C. Seligman, RNR) had swept for the mines, HMS Utmost was finally able to enter harbour at 1815 B/9 ending her 8th war patrol (6th in the Mediterranean). (5)

24 May 1941 Around 2000 B/24, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) departed Malta for her 9th war patrol (7th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the Gulf of Hammamet and to perform a special operation.
She was swept out until 2040 B/24 by HMS Swona (?). (5)

26 May 1941 In a signal timed 2139 B/26, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta informed HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) of the following; In view of recent enemy losses it seems probable he may take coast. You have complete liberty of action north-west of line 60° from Kuriat.

At 2300 B/26, in position 36°05'N, 10°36'E, HMS Utmost sighted a tanker of about 6000 tons bearing 360° at a range of 6 miles. Course of the tanker was 180°. She was burning lights and is thus thought to be a Vichy-French vessel which was indeed most likely the case. No Axis vessel is known to have been in the area at this time. (17)

27 May 1941 At 1430 B/27, in position 36°03'N, 10°34'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) sighted a vessel bearing 270° at a range of 5 miles. Course of this vessel was 160°. It was believed to be a Vichy-French sulphate vessel which was indeed most likely the case. No Axis vessel is known to have been in the area at this time.

At 2146 B/27, in position 35°58'N, 10°34'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) surfaced to carry out a special operation. No information appears to have survived but it probably was to land explosives and other stores for the Breuillac/Mounier network.

The special operation was completed around 2300 B/27 when HMS Utmost set course to patrol off Kuriat Island in accordance with the Senior Officer Submarine's Malta signals timed 2139 B/21 and 1336 B/22. (16)
28 May 1941 At 0330 B/28, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) sighted a vessel leaving Monastir and proceeding eastwards. The vessel was lighted so obviously Vichy-French. (5)

29 May 1941 HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) attacked a merchant vessel with three torpedoes about 5 nautical miles south-east of Kuriat. No hits were obtained. One of the torpedoes had a gyro failure. [The target has not yet been identified and was most likely Vichy-French.]
1140 B/29 - While in position 35°44'N, 11°09'E, sighted a Vichy-French destroyer of the Simoun-class bearing 270° at a range of 2.5 miles. The destroyers course was 185°. She was hugging the coast.

1350 B/29 - In position 35°43'N, 11°06'E sighted a merchant ship of about 3000 tons. One aircraft was circling overhead and was believed to be an escort. Range was 10000 yards. Closed at speed to attack. In the attack three torpedoes were fired from 4500 yards. Upon firing Utmost went to 80 feet. One of the torpedoes had a gyro failure and appeared to be cruising around in an erratic and alarming matter. After 10 minutes HE from the torpedo became fainter and finally disappeared.

At the time this was believed to have been the Italian Florida II (3100 GRT, built 1905) but she was at Sfax at the time. In a signal timed 2130 B/28, HMS Utmost was informed by the Senior Officer Submarines Malta of this ship at Sfax. The target has not yet been identified and was most likely Vichy-French. (5)
30 May 1941 At 0750 B/30, south of Pantelleria, in position 36°10'N, 11°57'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) heard faint HE on bearing 060°.

One minute later a merchant vessel of about 5000 tons escorted by a torpedo boat and a flying boat was sighted on the same bearing at a range of 6 miles. Enemy course was 150°. No attack was made as it was not possible to get within attacking range.
This was most likely the German transport Sparta (1724 GRT, built 1927) escorted by the torpedo boat Rosolino Pilo. They sailed from Trapani at 1730 B/29 and arrived at Tripoli at 1150 B/31. (5)

31 May 1941 At 0315 B/31, south of Pantelleria, in position 36°20'N, 11°52'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) sighted a darkened schooner bearing 150° at a range of 3 to 4 miles. No attack was made. (5)

31 May 1941 In a signal timed 0959 B/31, the following orders were signalled to HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) and HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) by the Senior Officer Submarines Malta; Leave patrol as follows to arrive Marsamxett 0730 B/4. Utmost P.M./2 through position 36°15'N, 12°50'E. Upright P.M./3 through position 35°51'N, 15°15'E and 36°00'N, 14°52'E. (18)
2 Jun 1941 At 2130 B/2, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) surfaced and left patrol to return to Malta. (5)

3 Jun 1941 At 1700 B/3, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) received the Senior Officer Submarines Malta signal timed 1225 B/3 which states the following; Remain within 20 miles radius of position 36°10'N, 13° 10'E to the south-west of line 115° through that position. At 0930 B/3 three cruisers and four destroyers had been sighted in position 36° 20'N, 12°10'E proceeding on course 030°. Also six destroyers and six merchant vessels were sighted in position 36°15'N, 12°15'E proceeding on course 190° 12 knots. Blenheims had been sent to attack. Convoy may return and cruisers may cover to eastward. Leave patrol P.M./4 to arrive at Malta at 0630 B/5.
This must have referred to a cover force made up of the Italian light cruisers Luigi di Savoia Duca Degli Abruzzi, Giuseppe Garibaldi and the destroyers Granatiere, Fuciliere, Bersagliere and Alpino.

They were covering a convoy made up of the Italian transports Aquitania (4971 GRT, built 1924), Beatrice C. (7015 GRT, built 1920), Caffaro (6476 GRT, built 1924), Montello (6117 GRT, built 1927), Nirvo (5270 GRT, built 1919) and the Italian tanker Poza Rica (7799 GRT, built 1940). The close escort of this convoy was made up of the destroyers Aviere, Camicia Nera, Geniere and Dardo. The torpedo boat Giuseppe Missori sailed from Tripoli at 1215 B/2 to meet the convoy and reinforce the escort.
The convoy was attacked in the afternoon by British aircraft during which the Montello was sunk and the Beatrice C. was heavily damaged and later scuttled as she could not be salvaged.

The convoy had sailed from Naples 1730 B/1 - 1930 B/1 and arrived at Tripoli at 1145 B/4 - 1237 B/4 (except the two transports which were sunk and Camicia Nera who returned to Naples after finishing off Beatrice C.. (5)
4 Jun 1941 At 2120 B/4, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) surfaced and left patrol to continue her passage to Malta. (5)
5 Jun 1941 At 0745 B/5, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) ended her 9th war patrol (7th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
[No logbook is available of HMS Utmost is available for the month of June 1941. Some details may thus be missing for this month.] (5)

17 Jun 1941 Around 1630 B/17, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) departed Malta for her 10th war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Tyrrhenian Sea to the north of the Straits of Messina (position 39°10'N, 15°56'E). She also was to destroy a railway if the opportunity arose.
En-route she was to pass to the south of Pantelleria and south of Ustica.
For the daily (incomplete though due to no log available for June 1941) and attack positions of HMS Utmost during this patrol see the map below.

21 Jun 1941 At 0120 B/21, north-west of Marittimo Island, in position 38°03'N, 11°59'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) sighted three unidentified ships bearing 180° at a range of 6 miles. Enemy course was 240°. HMS Utmost turned to attack but it was quickly broken off when it was realised the range was too great.

This were perhaps A/S vessels or minesweepers. The only movement of any consequence in this area is a convoy made up of salvage vessel Rostro (Italian, 333 GRT, built 1917), transport Pietro Querini (Italian, 1004 GRT, built 1906) and the schooner Ettoire Fieramosca leaving Trapani at 0515 B/21 for Pantelleria but the time does not match unless there is an error somewhere.
At 0555 B/21, smoke was sighted bearing 210°.
At 1050 B/21, in approximate position 38°16'N, 12°40'E, a small coastal steamer was sighted bearing 190°. The enemy was coast crawling eastwards. (5)

22 Jun 1941 At 2257 B/22, east-north-east of Stromboli Island, in position 38°50'N, 15°24'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) sighted an illuminated hospital ship bearing 160° at a range of 6 miles which looked like either one of the steamers Gerusalemme (Italian, 8052 GRT, built 1916) or Galilea (Italian, 8040 GRT, built 1916). Enemy course was 000°. Closed on the surface to investigate.

[We have been unable to identify this vessel, no hospital ship mentioned in this area (mostly operating in the Adriatic at that time). No northward traffic on the Messina-Naples route either. The Vichy-French hospital ship Canada was off Syria at this time so she was not in the area either.] (5)
23 Jun 1941 At 0800 B/23, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) proceeded to investigate the possibilities of blowing up an Italian west coast railway line between latitudes 39°10'N and 39°20'N.
At 1010 B/23, in position 39°06'N, 16°07'E the masts and funnels of minesweepers were seen bearing 095°. They were proceeding on course 000°. (5)

24 Jun 1941 At 0001 B/24, near position 39°10'N, 16°03'E (north of Amantea), HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) launched a folbot manned by Lt. D.R. Schofield, Royal Fusiliers and Lance Corporal F.C. Morgan to blow up a train in a railway tunnel.
2210 B/23 - Surfaced and proceeded towards the shore. A suitable tunnel in which to wreck a train had been spotted during the day.
0001 B/24 - Launched the Folbot with a party of two 400 yards from the shore. They successfully placed the charge and returned safely.
0200 B/24 - A train went through the tunnel but did not blow up.
0245 B/24 - Launched the Folbot again. The railway was now blown up without waiting for a train. The party returned safely shortly before dawn at 0500 B/24.
1315 B/24 - Received the Senior Officer Submarines Malta's signal timed 0934 B/24 ordering to patrol off the Straits of Messina. Course was set accordingly. (5)
25 Jun 1941 Around 0200 B/25, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) commenced patrol to the north of the Straits of Messina.
At 0700 B/25, in position 38°21'N, 15°35'E, a 1500 tons coastal passenger steamer was sighted bearing 250° at a range of 2 miles. Enemy course was 090°. [We have been unable to identify this vessel.]

At 0815 B/25, in position 38°19'N, 15°37'E, what is thought to be the Italian torpedo boat Albatros was sighted bearing 135° at a range of 3 miles. Enemy course was 315°. She later disappeared to the eastwards.
This was indeed Albatros which had sailed from Messina at 0715 B/25 for an A/S patrol north of the Strait and returned at 2115 B/25.

At 1120 B/25, in position 38°25'N, 15°44'E, a 6000 tons merchant vessel, partially laden was seen bearing 163° at a range of 5 miles. Enemy course was 020°. [We have been unable to identify this vessel.]

At 1350 B/25, in position 38°24'N, 15°28'E, a convoy of four merchant vessels and two destroyers was sighted bearing 230° at a range of 6 miles. Enemy course was 135°. They were considerably to the westward of the line on which it was anticipated they would approach Messina having made Cape Rasocolmo on a course of 135° but they altered course to round Cape Rasocolmo and the attack had to be broken off.

This convoy was made up of the Italian troop transports Oceania (19507 GRT, built 1933), Neptunia (19475 GRT, built 1932), Marco Polo (12272 GRT, built 1912) and Esperia (11398 GRT, built 1920) screened by the Italian destroyers Aviere (convoy leader), Geniere, Vincenzo Gioberti and Antonio Da Noli. They had sailed at 17.5 knots from Naples at 0230-0410 B/25 for Tripoli via the Straits of Messina and Taranto. At 1250 B/25, the torpedo boat Calliope sailed from Messina to reinforce the escort. The Third Cruiser Division was to provide cover. The convoy was detected by enemy aircraft and diverted to Taranto where it arrived at 1716-1938 B/26. It sailed again at 1600-1611 B/27 for Tripoli where it arrived at 1019-1217 B/29. The movement of this convoy was known through ULTRA, although it mistakenly identified Victoria instead of Esperia. [see files DEFE3/832/956, DEFE3/688/153, etc.].

At 2010 B/25, in position 38°21'N, 15°27'E, what is thought to be the Italian torpedo boat Albatros was sighted bearing 230° at a range of 5 miles. Enemy course was 160°. This was indeed Albatros, see above.

At 2230 B/25, course was set to the westwards to return to Malta in accordance with the Senior Officer Submarines Malta's signal timed 1824 B/25. [the patrol report gives the time of origin of this signal as 1824 B/25, the war diary summaries give the time of origin of this signal as 1842 B/25.] (5)

26 Jun 1941 HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian merchant Enrico Costa (4080 GRT, built 1928) 4 nautical miles from Cape Todaro, Sicily, Italy in position 38°07'N, 14°37'E. The vessel was on passage from Catania to Palermo with 3000 tons of coal. There were no casualties.
The Italian torpedo boats Castore and Albatros were directed to the scene to hunt the attacker.

1200 B/26 -Sighted a partly laden merchant ship of about 6000 tons bearing 060°, 5 nautical miles away. Utmost closed to 1100 yards and fired two torpedoes. One of the torpedoes hit amidships. The crew abandoned ship but it did not sink. Utmost later fired a third torpedo that hit and sank the ship.
1425 B/26 - In position 38°11'N, 14°21'E, sighted what is thought to be the Italian torpedo boat Albatros bearing 270° at a range of 3 to 4 miles. She appeared to be hunting.
1535 B/26 - In position 38°10'N, 14°17'E, sighted what is thought to be the Italian torpedo boat Albatros yet again bearing 255° at a range of 3 miles. Enemy course was 090°.

Both sightings were indeed Albatros. She had sailed from Messina at 0905 B/26 for an A/S search and later joined the torpedo boat Castore to hunt a submarine off Cape Orlando. She returned to Messina at 0605 B/27.
Between 1655 B/26 and 1710 B/26 four distant depth charge patters were heard. (5)
27 Jun 1941 Around 0700 B/27, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) proceeded to investigate the possibilities of blowing up another railway line in position 38°04'N, 14°08'E.
At 1250 B/27, in position 38°03'N, 14°07'E, two 'Albatros'-type torpedo boats were seen bearing 180° at a range of 5 miles. Enemy course was 090°.

The Albatros was not part of a class of ships and was 'one of a kind'. The destroyer Antonio Pigafetta was proceeding westwards from Taranto to Trapani where it arrived at 1715 B/27. Did she briefly turn back? We have no other destroyer/torpedo boat in this area at that time. The Italian torpedo boat Giuseppe Sirtori sailed from Palermo for Naples at 1855 B/27, but this was after the sighting.
At 2208 B/27, HMS Utmost proceeded to carry out a landing operation with the object of blowing up a train in a tunnel in position 38°03'N, 14°07'E.

At 0001 B/27, having closed to 600 yards from the landing place the current offset the submarine and half an hour was spent trying to find the tunnel. Finally the Folbot was launched but shortly afterwards someone ashore, presumably a sentry, started shouting and hailing at the top of his voice. Presumably the submarine had been sighted. Lt. Schofield and Lance Corporal Morgan had also heard the alarm and returned. The operation was then abandoned and HMS Utmost retired from the area. (5)

28 Jun 1941 At 1410 B/28, north-east of Palermo in position 38°15'N, 13°35'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN), sighted three cruisers and three destroyers bearing 230° at a range of 5 miles. Enemy course was 130° and later 090°. The nearest cruiser was a Condottieri-class, the others could not be identified. The submarine could not come within attacking range but attempted to make an enemy report, however Malta failed to acknowledge reception. These were most certainly the light cruiser Raimondo Montecuccoli and the destroyers Fuciliere and Bersagliere which reached Palermo at 1530 A/28. The other warships have not been identified (so far).

The light cruiser Raimondo Montecuccoli escorted by the torpedo boat Giacinto Carini had sailed from La Spezia at 1300-1315 B/27. Carini turned back during the night of 27/28 and reached La Spezia at 2035 B/28. In the morning of the 28th, the light cruiser was joined by the destroyers Bersagliere and Fuciliere which sailed from Palermo at 1952-2000 B/27. They arrived at Palermo at 1530-1555 B/28. No other escort listed or found in this area at the time. Note: the light cruisers Giuseppe Garibaldi and Giovanni Delle Bande Nere were in Palermo at the time but did not sortie.? (19)

1 Jul 1941 At 1630 B/1, west of Pantelleria, in position 36°50'N, 11°45'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) heard HE and on coming to periscope depth two Grecale-class destroyers were seen bearing 120° at a range of 4 miles. Enemy course was 175°.

These destroyers were probably from the escort of the Rialto-convoy which according to their sailing orders were to pass through 199° - Punta Polacca (Pantelleria) - 2.5 miles at 1615 B/1. The convoy was made up of the Italian transports Rialto (Italian, 6099 GRT, built 1927), Francesco Barbaro (Italian, 6343 GRT, built 1940), Barbarigo (Italian, 5293 GRT, built 1930), Sebastiano Venier (Italian, 6310 GRT, built 1940), Andrea Gritti (Italian, 6338 GRT, built 1939) and the Ankara (German, 4768 GRT, built 1937) screened by the Italian destroyers Freccia (convoy leader), Dardo, Strale and Turbine. They sailed from Naples at 1541-1800 B/30 June and arrived at Tripoli at 1752-1920 B/2. (5)
3 Jul 1941 HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) ended her 10th war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (5)
8 Jul 1941 HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) is docked in No.2 graving dock at the Malta Dockyard. (20)
12 Jul 1941 HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) is undocked. (20)

17 Jul 1941 Around 1315 B/17, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) departed Malta for her 11th war patrol (9th in the Mediterranean). On departure exercises were carried out until around 1730 B/17 (see below). She was ordered to patrol in the Tyrrhenian Sea to the north of the Straits of Messina, one of the four submarines deployed for operation SUBSTANCE (the others were HMS Urge and HMS Upholder from Malta and HMS P 32 from Gibraltar).
HMS Utmost was also to destroy a railway if the opportunity arose, just like in her previous patrol that was in the same area.

17 Jul 1941 In the afternoon, HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, RN), HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN), HMS Urge (Lt. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) conducted exercises off Malta with HMS Beryl (?). (21)
20 Jul 1941 HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) attacked a merchant vessel with two torpedoes north of Cape St. Vito in position 38°26'N, 12°48'E. No hits were obtained.

The target has not been identified for certain as the attack was not reported but may have been against the Italian merchant vessel Ettore (4270 GRT, built 1912) who had sailed at 1830 B/20 from Palermo for Algiers where she was to take on a cargo of phosphate.
We have been unable to identify an escort. No destroyer or torpedo boat was in this area at that time.
2305 B/20 - In position 38°26'N, 12°48'E, sighted a 6000 tons merchant vessel bearing 050° at a range of 5 miles. Closed to investigate at high speed.

2325 B/20 - Fired two torpedoes. The second torpedo wandered off to starboard. The first torpedo probably passed ahead of the target as the speed of the target had been over estimated. Turned away after firing.
2326 B/20 - Sighted an escorting destroyer coming straight towards but after a few minutes the enemy turned away to the westwards.
Passage through the Straits of Gibraltar of the eastbound convoy and sailing from Gibraltar of the remaining ships involved in the operation.

Around 0130 B/21 convoy WS 9C passed the Straits of Gibraltar. The convoy at that moment consisted of six merchant ships; City of Pretoria (8049 GRT, built 1937), Deucalion (7516 GRT, built 1930), Durham (10893 GRT, built 1934), Melbourne Star (11076 GRT, built 1936), Port Chalmers (8535 GRT, built 1933) and Sydney Star (11095 GRT, built 1936).

At the time they passed through the Straits they were escorted by HMS Nelson (Capt. T.H. Troubridge, RN), HMS Edinburgh (Capt. H.W. Faulkner, RN, flying the flag of Rear-Admiral E.N. Syfret, RN), HMS Manxman (Capt. R.K. Dickson, RN), HMS Lightning (Cdr. R.G. Stewart, RN), HMAS Nestor (Cdr. A.S. Rosenthal, RAN), HMS Avon Vale (Lt.Cdr. P.A.R. Withers, RN), HMS Eridge (Lt.Cdr. W.F.N. Gregory-Smith, RN) and HMS Farndale (Cdr. S.H. Carlill, RN).

HMS Manchester (Capt. H. Drew, DSC, RN), HMS Arethusa (Capt. A.C. Chapman, RN), HMS Cossack (Capt. E.L. Berthon, DSC and Bar, RN), HMS Maori (Cdr. R.E. Courage, DSO, DSC and Bar, RN), HMS Sikh (Cdr. G.H. Stokes, RN) departed Gibraltar around 0200 B/21 escorting troopship Leinster (4302 GRT, built 1937) which was to join the convoy. However Leinster grounded while leaving Gibraltar and had to left behind. The small fleet tanker RFA Brown Ranger (3417 GRT, built 1941, master D.B.C. Ralph) left Gibraltar around the same time escorted by the destroyer HMS Beverley (Lt.Cdr. J. Grant, RN).

About one hour later, around 0300 B/21, HMS Renown (Rear-Admiral R.R. McGrigor, RN, flying the flag of Vice-Admiral J.F. Somerville, KCB, DSO, RN), HMS Ark Royal (Capt. L.E.H. Maund, RN), HMS Hermione (Capt. G.N. Oliver, RN), HMS Faulknor (Capt. A.F. de Salis, RN), HMS Fearless (Cdr. A.F. Pugsley, RN), HMS Firedrake (Lt.Cdr. S.H. Norris, DSO, DSC, RN), HMS Foresight (Cdr. J.S.C. Salter, RN), HMS Forester (Lt.Cdr. E.B. Tancock, DSC and Bar, RN), HMS Foxhound (Cdr. G.H. Peters, DSC, RN), HMS Fury (Lt.Cdr. T.C. Robinson, RN) and HMS Duncan (Lt.Cdr. A.N. Rowell, RN) departed Gibraltar to give convoy for the convoy during the passage to Malta.
At sea the forces were redistributed; Force H, the cover force HMS Renown, HMS Nelson, HMS Ark Royal, HMS Hermione, HMS Faulknor, HMS Foresight, HMS Forester, HMS Fury, HMS Lightning and HMS Duncan.

Force X, the close escort for the convoy HMS Edinburgh, HMS Manchester, HMS Arethusa, HMS Manxman, HMS Cossack, HMS Maori, HMS Sikh, HMAS Nestor, HMS Fearless, HMS Firedrake, HMS Foxhound, HMS Avon Vale, HMS Eridge and HMS Farndale.
Plan for the operation

Force H was to cover the convoy until it reached the narrows between Sicily and Tunisia. Force X was to escort the convoy all the way to Malta. Ships of Force X also had troops for Malta on board that had been taken to Gibraltar by troopship Pasteur. On 23 July 1941, the day the eastbound convoy would reach ‘the narrows’ five empty transports and two tankers would depart Malta for Gibraltar (Convoy MG 1) The seven empty transports were; Group 1 (speed 17 knots) HMS Breconshire (9776 GRT, built 1939), Talabot (6798 GRT, built 1936),
Group 2 (speed 14 knots) Thermopylae (6655 GRT, built 1930), Amerika (10218 GRT, built 1930),

Group 3 (speed 12 knots) Settler (6202 GRT, built 1939), Tanker Svenor (7616 GRT, built 1931) and Tanker Hoegh Hood (9351 GRT, built 1936) These were escorted by the destroyer HMS Encounter (Lt.Cdr. E.V.St J. Morgan, RN) which had been repairing and refitting at Malta.

Through intelligence it was known that the Italian Navy had five battleships operational (three of them at Taranto) and about ten cruisers divided between Taranto, Palermo and Messina. The Italian Air Force had about 50 torpedo planes and 150 bombers (30 of which were dive bombers) stationed in Sardinia and Sicily, roughly half of each type on both islands.

The Royal Air Force was able to be of more help than during the previous convoy trip from Gibraltar to Malta last January. Aircraft from Gibraltar conducted A/S patrols for the fleet during the first two days of the passage to the east. Also patrols were flown between Sardinia and the coast of Africa, while aircraft from Malta conducted reconnaissance between Sardinia and Sicily, besides watching the Italian ports. Malta would also provide fighter escort for Force X and the convoy after Force H would part with them and HMS Ark Royal could no longer provide fighter cover for them.

During the operation eight submarines (HMS Olympus (Lt.Cdr. H.G. Dymott, RN), HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN), HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, RN), HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN), HMS Urge (Lt. E.P. Tomkinson, RN), HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN), HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) and HrMs O 21 (Lt.Cdr. J.F. van Dulm, RNN)) were on patrol to report and attack Italian warships that might be sailed to intercept the convoy.
The passage East, 22 July 1941

On 22 July the destroyers from Force X oiled from the Brown Ranger two at a time. A task that took about 10 hours. Having completed the oiling of the destroyers the Brown Ranger and her escort returned to Gibraltar. An Italian aircraft had reported Force H in the morning but the convoy and Force X, at that moment about 100 nautical miles to the south-westward, appeared not to have been sighed. At 2317 B/22 the Italian submarine Diaspro missed HMS Renown with torpedoes. HMAS Nestor sighted the torpedo tracks and was able to warn HMS Renown which was then able to avoid the torpedoes by doing an emergency turn to port.
The passage East and attacks by the Italian Air Force, 23 July 1941

Force H rejoined the convoy around 0800 B/23 as the British were now approaching the danger area. Shadowing aircraft had already reported the position of the fleet that morning and heavy air attacks soon followed.

The first came at 0945 hours, a well times combination of nine high level bombers and six or seven torpedo planes approaching from the north-east. HMS Ark Royal had eleven fighters up, which met the bombers about 20 miles from the fleet. They managed to down two of the nine bombers but unfortunately three Fulmars were shot down by the enemy. The other seven bombers came on working round the head of the screen of destroyers to attack the convoy from the starboard beam at a height of 10000 feet. Their bombs fell harmlessly amongst the leading ships as they altered course to avoid the attack. The torpedo planes however were more successful. They came from ahead out of the sun, flying low, and as the destroyers opened fire they divided into groups of two or three and to attack the convoy on both sides. Two aircraft attacked HMS Fearless, stationed ahead in the screen, dropping their torpedoes at ranges of 1500 and 800 yards from a height of 70 feet. The destroyer avoided the first torpedo, but was hit by the second, set on fire, and completely disabled. Other aircraft went to press on their attacks on the convoy itself. One of them, dropping its torpedo between two merchant vessels hit HMS Manchester as she was turning to regain her station after avoiding two torpedoes fired earlier. She reversed helm once more but to no avail. During the attacks three enemy torpedo bombers were shot down by AA fire from the ships.

HMS Manchester was badly damaged and could only use one engine out of four. At first she could steam only 8 knots. She was ordered to make for Gibraltar with HMS Avon Vale as escort. That evening, further to the westward, they were attacked again by three enemy torpedo planes but their AA gunfire kept the enemy at a distance. Both ships successfully reached Gibraltar on the 26th.

At 1010 B/23 five more bombers tried to attack the convoy crossing this time from north to south. Fighters from HMS Ark Royal forced them to drop their bombs from great height and mostly outside the screen.
At 1645 B/23 five more torpedo planes led by a seaplane came in from the northward. Three Fulmars caught them about 20 miles away. They managed to shoot down two planes and drove the remainder away.

Soon afterwards the fleet arrived off the entrance to the Skerki Channel. There HMS Hermione was transferred to Force X to take the place of HMS Manchester. Six destroyers were assigned to Force H and eight to Force X. At 1713 hours Vice-Admiral Somerville hauled round to the westward. HMS Ark Royal kept her Fulmars up until RAF Beaufighters had arrived from Malta to take over.

The convoy was attacked again around 1900 B/23. Four torpedo planes arrived from the eastward, flying low and and working round from ahead to the starboard side of the convoy. They approached in pairs in line abreast. They kept HMS Sikh (on the starboard bow of the screen) between them and their target until nearly the moment for attack, thereby hampering the AA fire from the other ships. They dropped their torpedoes from long range from a height of 50 feet and nearly hit HMS Hermione, sternmost ship in the starboard column. To avoid the attack each column of the convoy turned 90° outwards and all warships opened barrage fire from all guns that would bear. The barrage however fell short but it caused the Italians to drop their torpedoes early. Also one of the enemy was possibly shot down.

This attack scattered the convoy and it took some time to reform. At 1945 B/23 about seven bombers appeared from ahead at a height of about 14000 feet to attack the convoy from the port side. The convoy altered 40° to port together and the escort opened up a controlled fire with some hesitation as the Italian aircraft looked a lot like Beaufighters. The bombing was extremely accurate. Several bombs fell near HMS Edinburgh which was leading the port column, and a near miss abreast a boiler room disabled HMS Firedrake which had been sweeping ahead of the convoy. She could no longer steam so Rear-Admiral Syfret ordered her back to Gibraltar in tow of HMS Eridge. They had an anxious passage, being shadowed by aircraft continuously during daylight hours, but were not again attacked. On the 25th HMS Firedrake managed to lit one boiler so the tow was slipped. Both destroyers entered Gibraltar harbour on the 27th.

Soon after leaving the Skerki Channel in the evening of the 23th the convoy hauled up to the north-east towards the coast of Sicily. This was to lessen the danger of mines. The Italians did not shadow the convoy after the attack at 1945 hours and missed this alteration of course which they clearly did not expect. Around 2100 hours, as it was getting dark, enemy aircraft were seen searching along its old line of advance. During the evening the convoy sighted flares several times about 20 miles to the south.
Continued passage to the east and enemy attacks, 24 July 1941

Between 0250 and 0315 hours the convoy was however attacked by the Italian MAS boats MAS 532 and MAS 533. The managed to torpedo and damaged the Sydney Star. HMAS Nestor went alongside and took off almost 500 soldiers. Sydney Star was however able to continue her passage as staggler escorted initially by HMAS Nestor. Admiral Syfret however sent back HMS Hermione. At 1000 B/24 eight German dive bombers and two high level bombers attacked. Their bombs fell close the escorting ships. HMS Hermione shot down one dive bomber. The three ships arrived at Malta early in the afternoon.

The main body of the convoy meanwhile continued on its way unhindered after the attacks of the motor torpedo boats except for an attempt by three torpedo planes around 0700 hours. They dropped their torpedoes at a safe distance when fired on by the destroyers in the screen ahead. According to the orders Rear-Admiral Syfret was to leave the convoy now, if there was no threat from Italian surface forces, and go on to Malta with the cruisers and some of the destroyers. They were to land the passengers and stores, complete with fuel and return to Force H as soon as possible. The remaining destroyers were to accompany the transports to Malta. They too were to join Force H as soon as possible. Rear-Admiral Syfret felt easy about the surface danger as all Italian ships were reported in harbour the day before, but he was anxious about the threat to the convoy from the air. He decided to go ahead with the cruiser but leave all destroyers with the convoy so at 0745 B/24, HMS Edinburgh, HMS Arethusa and HMS Manxman left the convoy and pressed ahead at high speed to Malta where they arrived at noon the same day. The transports and the destroyers arrived about four hours later. They had been attacked only once by a torpedo plane since the cruisers separated.
Return passage of the warships of force X to make rendez-vous with Force H.

In the evening HMS Edinburgh, HMS Arethusa, HMS Hermione and HMS Manxman sailed together followed by five destroyers; HMS Cossack, HMS Maori, HMS Sikh, HMAS Nestor, HMS Foxhound, later the same evening. The destroyers overtook the cruisers in the morning of the 25th. The sixth destroyer, HMS Farndale, had to be left at Malta due to defects (condenser problems). All ships made rendez-vous with Force H to the north-west of Galita Island at 0800 B/25.
Movements of Force H after it parted from the convoy.

After parting with the convoy in the evening of the 23rd, Vice-Admiral Somerville had taken force H westward at 18 knots until the afternoon of the 24th going as far west as 03°30’E. He then turned back to meet Admiral Syfret, also sending from HMS Ark Royal six Swordfish aircraft which left her in position 37°42’N, 07°17’E at 1000 B/25. After their junction Forces H and X made the best of way towards Gibraltar. Fighter patrols of HMS Ark Royal shot down a shadowing aircraft soon after the fleet had shaped course to the westward, losing a Fulmar in doing so. However another aircraft had meanwhile reported the fleet.

High level bombers appeared from the east and torpedo bombers from the north at 1100 hours. HMS Ark Royal at that moment had four fighters in the air and sent up six more. They prevented the bombing attack shooting down three aircraft out of eight at a cost of two Fulmars, while the ships watched the enemy jettison their bombs 15 miles away. The torpedo attack came to nothing too for the enemy gave up the attempt and retired while still several miles from the fleet. Two days later, on the 27th, the fleet reached Gibraltar.
The movements of the seven empty ships coming from Malta.

Six of the transports / tankers left Malta for Gibraltar in the morning of the 23rd, escorted by HMS Encounter. The seventh ship, tanker Svenor grounded while leaving harbour and was held up for some hours. At dusk, when a few miles from Pantelleria, the six ships devided into pairs according to their speed. HMS Encounter initially escorted the middle pair but joined the leading ships in the evening of the 24th when past the Galita Bank.

Italian aircraft, both high level bombers and torpedo planes, attacked all these ships on the 24th to the southward of Sardinia. They made their first attempt on the second pair of transports and HMS Encounter. Four torpedo planes attacked at 1230 B/24 and four bombers at 1250 B/24. No ships were hit though the bombs fell close. Next came the turn for the leading pair, which were attacked further westwards by two bombers that came singly at 1330 B/24 and 1400 B/24. The second plane nearly hit HMS Breconshire. Finally when the third pair of ships reached about the same position in the evening they were attacked by torpedo planes and the Hoegh Hood was damaged but she managed to arrive at Gibraltar only a few hours after her consort on the 27th. The last ship, the one that had been delayed at Malta, arrived on the 28th. (22)

22 Jul 1941 At 0645 B/22, west of Cape Rasocolmo, Sicily, in position 38°16'N, 15°26'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) sighted a tug towing a floating crane bearing 120° at a range of 5 miles. Enemy course was 260°.
At 1125 B/22, in position 38°15'N, 15°36'E, two trawlers were seen sweeping bearing 160° at a range of 6 miles. Enemy course was 290°.
[We have not been able to identify any of the vessels sighted on this day.] (5)

23 Jul 1941 At 0325 B/23, north-west of Cape Rasocolmo, Sicily, in position 38°23'N, 15°25'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) sighted a small coastal steamer bearing 050° at a range of 5 miles. Enemy course was 070°.
At 0605 B/23, in position 38°22'N, 15°33'E, a high-speed motor launch or motor torpedo boat was sighted bearing 090° at a range of 2 miles. Enemy course was 330° at a speed of 25 to 30 knots.

At 0630 B/23, in position 38°23'N, 15°30'E, a hospital ship, believed to be the italian Gradisca (13868 GRT, built 1913), was sighted bearing 350° at a range of 6 miles. Enemy course was 170°. She was subsequently joined by a small armed coaster off Cape Rasocolmo and they then proceeded towards the Straits of Messina in company.
