The only movement of an Italian hospital ship in Sicilian waters on that day is Arno (7785 GRT, built 1912) sailing from Syracuse at 2120 B/23 for Porto Empedocle. She had sailed from Naples at 0020 B/17 July for Syracuse. Unfortunately, the Supermarina war diaries do not give her date of arrival. Normally, she ought to have arrived at her destination on 18 July at the latest. Could she have been delayed and transited the Straits of Messina a few hours earlier on 23 July? It is a very wild guess. On the other hand, it would not be the first time that HMS Utmost reported sighting a hospital ship when there is doubt one was in the area (see 22 June).

However, two days later, to the south-east of Crete, HMS Thrasher (Lt.Cdr. P.J. Cowell, DSC, RN) sighted the eastbound Vichy-French hospital ship Canada (9684 GRT, built 1912). Perhaps this was the hospital ship seen by HMS Utmost. (5)

24 Jul 1941 At 1815 B/24, north of the Straits of Messina, in position 38°25'N, 15°43'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) sighted a sloop type escort vessel of about 1200 tons bearing 100° at a range of 4 miles. Enemy course was 210°. [We have been unable to identify this vessel.] (5)

25 Jul 1941 At 1515 B/25, just west of the northern entrance to the Straits of Messina, in position 38°19'N, 15°36'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) sighted a tug towing 5 targets for gunnery exercises bearing 115° at a range of 6 miles. Enemy course was due east, 290°.

The tow rope was long and it was thought shore batteries would open fire soon. As the tug passed close HMS Utmost went to 60 feet. Half an hour later she returned to periscope depth but the targets were untouched. [We have been unable to identify this tug.] (5)

26 Jul 1941 At 1605 B/26, just west of the northern entrance to the Straits of Messina, in position 38°17'N, 15°36'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) sighted a 1000 tons coaster bearing 190° at a range of 5 miles. Enemy course was to the north-west, 310°. [We have been unable to identify this vessel.] (5)

27 Jul 1941 HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) landed a sabotage party (Lt. D.R. Schoffield, Royal Fusiliers, and Lance Corporal F.C. Morgan) to blow up a railway and a train in position 38°47'N, 16°06'E (near Pizzo, Calabria, Italy). The rear end of a train was derailed.
At 0830 B/27 - north-east of Cape Vaticano, in position 38°48'N, 15°55'E, three trawlers were sighted, probably sweeping, bearing 055° at a range of 3 miles. Enemy course was 235°
0927 B/27 - Proceeded to investigate the railway on the south side of the Gulf of Euphemia in the vicinity of position 38°47'N, 16°06'E.

1400 B/27 - In position 38°45'N, 16°03'E, sighted two groups of three trawlers each proceeding towards. Bearings were 300° and 330°. It was at first thought the submarine had been spotted by aircraft but after worming a way through the two groups it was realised that they were heading towards Port St. Venere (now called Vibo Marina).
2330 B/27 - Landed the Folbot with a party of two which successfully laid explosives underneath a railway line.

0100 B/8 - A large explosion was seen as a large southbound train passed over the spot where the charge had been placed. Shortly afterwards the Folbot party returned safely. According to Italian sources the rear of the train was derailed. (5)

28 Jul 1941 HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian merchant vessel Frederico (1488 GRT, built 1920) in the southern Tyrrhenian Sea north-west of Paola, Italy in position 39°28'N, 15°52'E.
Frederico was escorted by the armed merchant cruiser Adriatico (1976 GRT, built 1931) who rescued all the crew except one.
1150 B/28 - West of Paola, Cosenza, in position 38°21'N, 16°00'E, sighted two trawlers bearing 060° at a range of 6 miles. Enemy course was 180°.

1835 B/28 - Sighted a 4000 tons merchant ship escorted by a 5000 tons armed merchant cruiser bearing 330°, 9 nautical miles, course 160°, speed 8 knots. Started attack in which two torpedoes were fired from 700 yards. One hit was obtained and the ship sank immediately. The Armed Merchant Cruiser counter attacked with 20 depth charges but none were close.
According to Italian sources Frederico had been escorted by the armed merchant cruiser Adriatico (1976 GRT, built 1931) who rescued all the crew except one. (5)

30 Jul 1941 At 1401/30, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) received the Senior Officer Submarines Malta's signal timed 1110 B/30 which ordered her to patrol within 30 miles from Palermo and leave patrol P.M. on 31 July. Course was set for the Palermo area to comply with these orders. (5)

31 Jul 1941 At 0620 B/31, just east-north-east of Cape Gallo, in position 38°14'N, 13°22'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) sighted a laden merchant vessel of about 5000 tons bearing 270° at a range of 7 miles. Enemy course was 120°. No attack could be made due to the long range.

The small tanker Irma (Italian, 305 GRT, built 1908) sailed from Tapani for Palermo at 2030 B/30. However, the size does not match what Lt.Cdr. Cayley saw. We have been unable to place other vessels in this area at this time.
At 1030 B/31, a small auxiliary craft was seen to be sweeping near Cape Gallo.
At 2124 B/31, HMS Utmost surfaced and left patrol to return to Malta. (5)

2 Aug 1941 At 0505 B/2, south-east of Marittimo Island, in position 37°51'N, 11°59'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) sighted two Grecale-class destroyers bearing 160° at a range of 5 miles. Enemy course is 330° at 22 knots.
These were the Italian torpedo boats Centauro and Cigno sailed from Trapani at 2110-2115 B/1 for an A/S sweep and returned at 0650-0710 B/2. (5)
3 Aug 1941 Around 0800 B/3, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) ended her 11th war patrol (9th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (5)

19 Aug 1941 Around 1845 B/19, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) departed Malta for her 12th war patrol (10th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off south coast of Calabria, Italy. Once again she carried a raiding party to destroy a railway if the opportunity arose.
On departure from Malta exercises were carried out. Presumably with launching and recovering the Folbot boat which was to be used by the raiding party.
On departure from Malta the orders were to patrol off the Calabrian Coast but to remain south of 40°00'N.
21 Aug 1941 Around 1330 B/21, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) entered her patrol area. (5)

21 Aug 1941 In a signal timed 1415 B/21, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta informed HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN), HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) that a convoy of three Vichy-French ships would pass through position 41°35'N, 10°00'E about 0001/21 and would then proceed towards the Messina Strait. They were expected to pass through position 37°03'N, 16°46'E around 2000 B/22. They were not to be attacked.

This referred to the Vichy-French evacuation convoy 'C' which was made up of the liners Marrakech (6288 GRT, built 1914), Sinaia (8567 GRT, built 1922) and Groix (9957 GRT, built 1922). This convoy had departed Toulon on 19 August and arrived at Beirut on 28 August. At Beirut Marrakech embarked 733 passengers, Sinaia embarked 1867 passengers and Groix embarked 1562 passengers. Total number of passengers was 4162 of which 3674 were military personnel. The convoy departed Beirut on 29 August and arrived at Marseille on 5 September. (23)

23 Aug 1941 At 1115 B/23, while north of the Neto river estuary, in position 39°15'N, 17°09'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) sighted two trawlers, minesweeping, bearing 330° at a range of 5 miles. Enemy course was 180°.
At 1315 B/23, in position 39°25'N, 17°12'E, sighted one trawler, minesweeping, bearing 330° at a range of 5 miles. Enemy course was 180°. [We have been unable to identify this trawler.]

At 1600 B/23, in position 39°25'N, 17°12'E, sighted a schooner bearing 180° at a range of 2 miles. The enemy had hove to. This was perhaps the Triglav (ex-Yugoslav, 231 GRT, built 1920) which arrived at Crotone from Taranto at 1750 B/23.
At 1615 B/23, in position 39°25'N, 17°15'E, a tug towing a small seaplane dock was sighted bearing 270° at a range of 2 miles. Enemy course was 180°. [We have been unable to identify this tug.]

At 1635 B/23, in position 39°20'N, 17°10'E, a tug towing a lighter was seen bearing 220° at a range of 5 miles. Enemy course was 360°. This must have been the Valente (Italian, ex-French Michel Venture, 285 GRT, built 1916). She had sailed from Crotone at 1330 B/23 for Taranto, towing a pontoon and a lighter.

At 2250 B/23, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta's signal timed 1925 B/25 was received. This ordered her to patrol in position 39°07'N, 17°20'E as it was expected enemy heavy units may return to Taranto soon. [No Italian heavy units were returning to Taranto though, they were either going to Palermo or Cagliari.]
Lt.Cdr. Cayley, postponed the intended train wrecking operation and set course to comply with the orders received. (5)

24 Aug 1941 At 0205 B/24, in position 39°07'N, 17°20'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) sighted a small unidentified vessel, most likely a motor torpedo boat bearing 200° at a range of 2 miles. Evasive action was taken but Utmost did not dive. (5)
25 Aug 1941 At 1800 B/25, in position 38°58'N, 17°14'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) sighted a trawler bearing 210° at a range of 5 miles. Enemy course was 010°.
At 1815 B/25, in position 39°05'N, 17°08'E, a merchant vessel was sighted bearing 280° at a range of 6 miles. The vessel was in/off Crotone harbour.
At 1900 B/25, the merchant vessel left Crotone harbour and proceeded northwards while coast crawling. The vessel was of about 4000 tons and fully laden.

This must have been the Italian transport Honestas (4959 GRT, built 1920) which sailed from Crotone at 1855 B/25 for Taranto where she arrived at 0850 B/26 before proceeding to Bari and eventually Venice.

At 2239 B/25, in position 39°07'N, 17°22'E, a small unidentified vessel was seen, probably a motor torpedo boat, bearing 360° at a range of only one mile. HMS Utmost dived to evade surfacing again 50 minutes later. (5)

26 Aug 1941 In a signal timed 1253 B/26, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta signalled the following orders to HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN); Leave present position P.M./26 and carry out special operation and leave patrol P.M./28 returning by reverse of outward route to arrive Malta before 2030 B/30 or at 0700 B/31. (23)
27 Aug 1941 HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) landed a sabotage party (Lt. R. Wilson, R.A. and Marine W.G. Hughes) near the mouth of the river Saracena (near position 39°50'N, 16°31'E).
According to Italian sources the explosion did not destroy the bridge but caused enough damage requiring repairs before traffic could be resumed.
1200 B/27 - Started examining the coastline to find a suitable landing spot for a railway sabotage mission.
1430 B/27 - Hit bottom in position 39°49'N, 16°32'1"E while investigation a position for the landing. The chart shows 15 fathoms but silting from the river has probably occurred since the survey.
1440 B/27 - Went astern and slid off into depp water before continuing the investigation of the coastline.
2325 B/27 - Landed the Folbot with a party of two which successfully laid explosives on the railway bridge across the river Seracina.
0218 B/28 - A large explosion occurred. It appeared the charges were well placed.
0230 B/28 - The Folbot party returned safely. (5)

28 Aug 1941 HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) attacked an enemy merchant vessel in position 39°16'N, 17°17'E (near Punta Alice, Calabria, Italy). Three torpedoes were fired but no hits were obtained.

The target was the steamer Numidia (Italian, 5339 GRT, built 1913). With steamer Dielpi (Italain 1257 GRT, built 1912 screened by the armed merchant cruiser Piero Foscari (3423 GRT, built 1928), they sailed from Bari at 1330 B/27 for Naples via Messina. Dielpi was ordered to proceed to Taranto where she arrived at 1835 B/28. The two other ships went on to Messina where they arrived at 1100 B/29.

Immediately following this attack another escorted merchant vessel was seen but it could not be attacked. This was most probably the merchant vessel Dea Mazzella (3082 GRT, built 1919) escorted by the auxiliary Maria di Meglio on passage from Taranto to Crotone. A week later, the Dea Mazzella was damaged by air attack at Crotone.

0550 B/28 - In position 39°40'N, 16°51'E, sighted a Navigatori-class destroyer bearing 060° at a range of 4 miles. Enemy course was 330°. At the same time a Navigatori-class destroyer and a General-class torpedo boat was sighted bearing 030° at a range of 3 miles. Enemy course was 330°.
0630 B/28 - The three destroyers / torpedo boats altered course towards Taranto.

They were the destroyers Nicolò Zeno and Alvise Da Mosto and the torpedo boat Antonio Mosto. They had sailed from Trapani at 2044-2120 B/25 (the two destroyers) and Messina (the torpedo boat) at 1620 B/26. The destroyer Da Mosto took the torpedo boat Mosto in tow. They proceeded via Crotone (1325 B-1900 B/27) and arrived at Taranto at 1340-1630 B/28.
1615 B/28 - In position 39°24'N, 17°12'E, sighted a motor torpedo boat bearing 360° at a range of 3 miles. Enemy course was 130°. She disappeared to the northward 20 minutes later.

1650 B/28 - When in position 014°, 3 nautical miles from Alice Point, sighted an armed merchant cruiser bearing 060°, range 5 nautical miles. She was escorting a merchant vessel of about 6000 tons. Their course was about 260°. The armed merchant cruiser soon altered course to the south and came towards. It was expected that the merchant ship would follow but she did not do so immediately. Eventually Utmost had to go deep to avoid the armed merchant cruiser. When she returned to periscope depth it was noticed that the merchant ship had finally changed course towards. Started attack.
1724 B/28 - Fired three torpedoes from 3000 yards which all missed.

Shortly after firing, another merchant vessel of 4000 tons escorted by a schooner appeared from the northward. Utmost reloaded the tubes but the target disappeared in a rain squall before an attack could be carried out. (5)
29 Aug 1941 At 0821 B/29, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) picked up HE on bearing 210°.

At 0830 B/29, in position 38°11'N, 17°00'E, HMS Utmost returned to periscope depth. A Cavour-class battleship escorted by five destroyers was seen bearing 140° at a range of 6 miles. HE indicated that their speed was 20 knots.

Course was altered to 060° and closed at high speed for 10 minutes on a 110° track, hoping that the battleship might zig to port and come within range. The next periscope observation showed the battleship well out of range. The attack was broken off and an aircraft then forced HMS Utmost to go deep.

On return to periscope depth another Cavour-class battleship was seen hull down, 4 miles to the eastward of the first battleship sighted. Again HMS Utmost was forced deep by aircraft and an enemy report could not be made until 0935 B/29. Two attempts to pass the message were however unsuccessful.

This was the battleship Giulio Cesare which sailed from La Spezia at 1900-2010 B/27 screened by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta, Giovanni da Verazzano and Lanzerotto Malocello. They were reinforced by the destroyer Ugolino Vivaldi which sailed from Naples at 0610 B/28. They arrived at Taranto at 1745-1802 B/29. No second battleship or 5th destroyer were present.
At 0915 B/29, HMS Utmost left patrol to return to Malta. (5)

30 Aug 1941 At 04451 B/30, in position 37°05'N, 16°31'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) sighted the Vichy-French hospital ship Sphinx (11375 GRT, built 1915) bearing 130° at a range of 6 miles on course 310°. She was brightly illuminated. (5)
31 Aug 1941 Around 0825 B/31, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) ended her 12th war patrol (10th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (5)

12 Sep 1941 Around 1700 B/12, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) departed Malta for her 13th war patrol (10th in the Mediterranean). She had just returned to port after exercising in the morning when she was ordered to proceed to position 34°36'N, 12°12'E, near the Kerkennah shallows, to pick up the crew from a crashed Blenheim bomber.
Earlier in the afternoon exercises had been carried out of Malta. These included practice attacks on HMS Beryl (Mid. H.W. du Boisson, RNR) and also gunnery exercises. (5)
12 Sep 1941 For the daily positions of HMS Utmost during her 13th war patrol see the map below.

13 Sep 1941 At 0745 B/13, in position 34°35'N, 12°16'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) sighted distress signals bearing 220° distant 3 miles. Five Minutes later a rubber dinghy was sighted. The dinghy had three occupants. They were the missing aircrew and were quickly picked up. (5)
14 Sep 1941 Around 0800 B/14, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) ended her 13th war patrol (11th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (5)

18 Sep 1941 At 1630 B/18, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) departed Malta for her 14th war patrol (12th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the southern Tyrrhenian Sea. Once again she carried a raiding party (Lt. R. Wilson, Royal Artillery and Marine Hughes) to destroy a railway if the opportunity arose.

19 Sep 1941 In a signal timed 1703 B/19, Capt. S.10 informed HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) that the Vichy-French Hospital Ship Canada (9684 GRT, built 1912) and liner (troop transport) Colombie (13391 GRT, built 1931) were proceeding from position 41°35'N, 10°00'E at 1500 B/18 and 19th respectively and passes Messina 1200 B/20 and 1200 B21. These ships, if encountered, are not to be molested.

At 2134 B/19, HMS Utmost received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2052 B/19 which ordered her to patrol north-west of Marittimo in position 38°10'N, 11°46'E. One troopship (liner) escorted by one cruiser and two destroyers were expected around 1000 B/20 on course 038° at a speed of 22 knots. Leave position in execution of previous orders at 1800 B/20. Primary object is the troopship.

The signal referred to the troop transport Vulcania (Italian, 24469 GRT, built 1928) that was to proceed from Tripoli to Naples escorted by the Italian destroyers Nicoloso da Recco, Antonio da Noli, Antoniotto Usodimare and Emanuele Pessagno. (24)

20 Sep 1941 In a signal timed 1257 B/20, the Capt. S.10, referring to his signal timed 2052 B/19, that the speed of the enemy ship may be as low as 13 knots. HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) was ordered to remain in position until 2359 B/20 unless sighted earlier.

In a signal timed 1340 B/20, Capt. S.10 informed HMS Utmost that air reconnaissance reported one large liner close off Kelibia Roads at 0945 B/20 proceeding northwards at 17 knots escorted by 3 destroyers. The ship was already damaged on starboard side. [The ship was not damaged though, a torpedo attack by HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) had failed although the British thought a hit had been obtained.]

At 1420 B/20, north-north-west of Marittimo, in position 38°40'N, 11°40'E, HMS Utmost sighted a large merchant vessel (liner according to the logbook) and two masts bearing 320° at a range of 9 miles. Enemy course was 040°.

This was the troop transport Vulcania (Italian, 24469 GRT, built 1928) that was to proceed from Naples to Tripoli escorted by the Italian destroyers Nicoloso da Recco, Antonio da Noli, Antoniotto Usodimare and Emanuele Pessagno. (19)

22 Sep 1941 HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) landed a sabotage party (Lt. R. Wilson, R.A. and Marine W.G. Hughes) near Intavolata (position 39°29'N, 15°58'E). They were not successful in destroying the Torre di Rienzo railway tunnel as they were detected by Italian guards.

The operation was thwarted by the arrival of the Italian Militia (Carabiniere Alfonso Cappuziello, Corporal Francesco Scarnati and a railway watchman). The commandos fired three rounds with their pistols and Cappuziello fired back two rounds with his rifle. The commandos fled, abandoning their explosives. The Italian High Command was upset that reaction had not been more energetic and the two soldiers and three of their superior officers were sentenced to several days arrest.
1335 B/22 - Proceeded to investigate the coastline for the train wrecking operation.
2100 B/22 - Surfaced and closed the railway tunnel in position 39°29'N, 15°58'E.

2200 B/22 - Stopped in 14 fathoms of water, 1500 yards from the shore. Landed the Folbot with a party of two which were to lay explosives in the entrance to a railway tunnel on the busy Naples-Messina line.
0152 B/23 - The operation had to be abandoned as the raiding party was detected by Italian guards.
0210 B/23 - The Folbot party returned safely despite being under constant gunfire from the shore. (19)

23 Sep 1941 HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) again landed a sabotage party party (Lt. R. Wilson, R.A. and Marine W.G. Hughes) near position 39°04.5'N, 16°06'E (Campomara di San Giovanni). They were not successful in destroying a railway bridge over the river Oliva as, yet again, they were detected by Italian guards.

Again the operation was thwarted by two sentries Alfonso Sementa and Enrico Murgano. Apparently the commandos fired two pistol shots hitting Murgano in the right arm but both Italian soldiers returned fire forcing the two commandos to flee, they managed to return to the submarine. The alarm had been given and more Italian soldiers arrived, the area was thoroughly searched and the explosives discovered.
1600 B/23 - Sighted a three span railway bridge over the river Oliva in position 39°04'N, 16°06'E.
2100 B/23 - Surfaced and closed the railway bridge.
2145 B/23 - Stopped in 16 fathoms of water, 1400 yards from the shore. Landed the Folbot with a party of two which were to lay explosives on the railway bridge over the river Oliva.
2245 B/23 - Saw flashes from gunfire on the shore. Looked like the raiding party was again detected.
2300 B/23 - The raiding party returned safely. Utmost then retired to seaward. (19)

24 Sep 1941 At 0930 B/24, north-east of Cape Vaticano, in position 38°42'N, 15°55'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) sighted three minesweepers bearing 350° at a range of 6 miles. Enemy course was 150°.
At 1145 B/24, the minesweepers altered course to 180° to pass to the west of Cape Vaticano.
At 2010 B/24, north-west of Cape Vaticano, in position 38°44'N, 15°37'E, sighted a hospital ship bearing 355° at a range of 6 miles. Her course was 195°. (19)

25 Sep 1941 At 0700 B/25, west of Cape Vaticano, in position 38°36'N, 15°37'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) sighted two laden merchant ships of about 5000 tons escorted by an armed merchant cruiser bearing 048° at a range of 6 miles. Enemy course was 190°. Closed on a 120° track at high speed for 30 minutes but Utmost failed to get within a reasonable firing range so the attack was broken off.

These were the transports Capo Pino (Italian, 4785 GRT, built 1923) and Rubicone (Italian, 4965 GRT, built 1906) escorted by the Italian armed merchant cruiser Città di Tunisi (5419 GRT, built 1930). They had sailed from Naples at 0800 B/24 and arrived at Messina at 1030 B/25.

At 0830 B/25, In position 38°22'N, 15°45'E, sighted a merchant vessel bearing 180° at a range of 7 miles. Enemy course was 090°. Closed at high speed for 20 minutes on a 120° track. The attack had to be broken off when the enemy altered course towards the Straits of Messina.
Perhaps the Italian auxiliary Liv (3068 GRT, built 1896) which sailed from Milazzo and arrived at Messina at 1155 B/25.
At 1000 B/25, in position 38°23'N, 15°44'E, a torpedo boat and a schooner were sighted bearing 150° at a range of 5 miles. Their course was 010°.

At 1145 B/25, in position 38°18'N, 15°43'E, a 4000-ton merchant vessel was sighted bearing 180° at a range of 5 miles. Its course was 010°. Started an attack but the attack had to be broken off when the merchant vessel altered course to the westward 15 minutes later.
If there was an error in arrival time at Messsina this may have been the Liv (see above).

At 1345 B/25, in position 38°28'N, 15°44'E, sighted a tug with a lighter in tow bearing 000° at a range of 5 miles. Their course was 180°. Also three minesweepers were seen in position 38°24'N, 15.36'E. Range to these minesweepers was 5 miles on bearing 290°. Their course was 100°. [We have been unable to identify these vessels.]

At 1545 B/25, in position 38°19'N, 15°42'E, sighted an empty tanker of about 5000 tons escorted by a destroyer bearing 170° at a range of 3 miles. Their course was 015°. Turned to a 110° track prepared to fire four torpedoes from 900 yards from her port side. The destroyer however had other plans and suddenly increased speed and draw ahead on her port side. She approached to less than 100 yards from the submarine about the same time as the D.A. was coming on. The D.A. was missed as the target was obscured by the destroyer and due to the fact that the oily calmness of the sea made periscope observations very tricky at such short range.

This was probably the Italian transport Cesco (6161 GRT, built 1917) escorted by the torpedo boat Generale Achille Papa which sailed from Messina at 1400 B/25 at 8.5 knots and arrived at Naples at 1520 B/26. The Cesco was an engine-aft ship and looked like a tanker.
At 1730 B/25, in position 38°21'N, 15°41'E, sighted a patrol vessel bearing 100° at a range of 3 miles proceeding on a course of 270°. [We have been unable to identify this vessel.]

Note: it is a bit surprising that HMS Utmost did not sight the convoy made up of the transports Pulgliola (Italian, 2074 GRT, built 1917) and Marte (Italian, 5290 GRT, built 1917) escorted by the torpedo boat Generale Achille Papa which sailed from Palermo at 1030 B/24 and arrived at Messina at 1050-1130 B/25. Perhaps this convoy passed close to the land and were thus obscured by the land background. (19)
26 Sep 1941 At 0230 B/26, in position 38°30'N, 15°42'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) sighted two unidentified vessels bearing 250° at a range of 6 miles. Their course was 020°.
At 0900 B/26, in position 38°20'N, 15°45'E, sighted a torpedo boat and two minesweepers bearing 140° at a range of 5 miles.

Possibly the torpedo boat was the submarine chaser Albatros which was on A/S search. She sailed from Messina at 1900 B/25 and returned at 1500 B/26. We have not been able to identify the minesweepers.
At 0910 B/26, in position 38°22'N, 15°45'E, sighted a torpedo boat with a minesweeper astern of the ships sighted ten minutes before.
No other 'torpedo boat', besides Albatros should be in the area though.
The torpedo boat and the two minesweepers were seen to alter course to 270°.
At 1315 B/26, in position 38°22'N, 15°36'E, sighted a torpedo boat and two minesweepers at a range of 4 miles. Their course was 090°.
Possibly this was Albatros again.
At 1415 B/26, in position 38°22'N, 15°41'E, a tug and a lighter were seen bearing 270° at a range of 5 miles. Enemy course was 090°.
We have been unable to identify this tug.

At 1435 B/26, in position 38°18'N, 15°41'E, sighted three cruisers and eight destroyers bearing 170° at a range of 6 miles. Their course was 350°. The sea was oily calm. Manoeuvred to fire four torpedoes in a dispersed salvo on a 110° track at the third cruiser from a position of 2500 yards on her port side.
The three cruisers were in line ahead steaming at 28 knots and the eight destroyers were spread on either side of the cruisers in a 'V' shaped screen.

The port wing destroyer appeared as if she would pass about 500 yards away, between HMS Utmost and the third cruiser. In point of fact, she was still not in station and swerved to port at 30 knots missing HMS Utmost by a short margin. The noise of propellers sounded as if she had passed over the fore casing. It was realised that the D.A. was just coming on about this time, so wheel was put hard to port in the hope that the D.A. might still be on when the periscope was raised again. As soon as the destroyer had passed, the periscope was raised but unfortunately, the D.A. had been missed and could not be caught up with owing to the high speed of the enemy force.

The heavy cruiser Trieste screened by the destroyers Corazziere, Carabiniere, Ascari, Lanciere, Aviere and Camicia Nera sailed from Taranto at 2200-2300 B/25 for La Spezia but was diverted as Operation HALBERD unfolded and after patrolling in the Tyrrhenian Sea. At 1410 B/26, the heavy cruisers Trento and Gorizia sailed from Messina to join the Trieste squadron while it passed the Straits and operate against the HALBERD convoy. The squadron, minus Aviere and Camicia Nera, see below, reached La Maddalena at 0130-0416 B/29. [The number of escorting destroyers appears to have been six and not eight].
At 1508 B/26, HMS Utmost attempted to pass an enemy report but there was no reply. The broadcast was repeated twice to no avail.
At 1538 B/26, the W/T mast had to be lowered as an auxiliary patrol vessel was sighted bearing 180° at a range of 3 miles. The vessel was proceeding on a course of 020°.
At 1643 B/26, in position 38°33'N, 15°41'E, sighted two Grecale-class destroyers bearing 340° at a range of 6 miles proceeding on course 180°.

The were most probably the destroyers Aviere and Camicia Nera which reached Messina at 1800-1830 B/26. Apparently they had turned back having escorted the squadron in the area immediately to the north of the Straits of Messina. (19)

27 Sep 1941 At 0550 B/27, in position 38°27'N, 15°45'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) sighted a tug towing a lighter on bearing 055° at a range of 2 miles. Enemy course was 170°. We have been unable to identify this tug.
At 0815 B/27, in position 38°22'N, 15°36'E, a torpedo boat was sighted bearing 270° at a range of 5 miles. Enemy course was 090°. The enemy altered course to the northward 15 minutes later.

This was most probably the submarine chaser Albatros which was sent out to escort the German submarine U-371 across the Straits of Messina. Albatros was however sunk by HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) at 0902 B/27. It is rather odd that Upright and Utmost operated so closely to each other and that Utmost did not report any explosion when the submarine chaser was torpedoed.
At 0900 B/27, in position 38°20'N, 15°43'E, sighted a torpedo boat and two minesweepers bearing 160° at a range of 5 miles. Their course was 000°.

The Italian torpedo boat San Martino, sailed from Trapani at 2137 B/26 and arrived in Messina at 0920 B/27 but the position and course do not match. No other torpedo boats or destroyers, besides the submarine chaser (small torpedo boat) Albatros (see above) and the Generale Antonino Cascino (see below), were in the area.
At 0930 B/27, in position 38°23'N, 15°48'E, sighted a destroyer and another escort vessel, with a flying boat as air escort, bearing 080° at a range of 5 miles. Their course was 190°.

Most probably these were torpedo boat Generale Antonino Cascino and the minelayer Vieste (former German M 119, 515 tons, built 1918) which sailed from Naples at 1450 B/26 and arrived in Messina at 1140 B/27.

At 1040 B/27, in position 38°17'N, 15°41'E, sighted a 2000 tons merchant vessel escorted by an armed merchant cruiser bearing 180° at a range of 5 miles. Their course was 020°. Started an attack but at 1055 B/27, they altered course to the westward and the attack had to be broken off.

These were most probably the tanker Lina (Italian, 1235 GRT, built 1909) escorted by the auxiliary Lago Tana (783 GRT, built 1940). They sailed at 0930 B/27 from Messina for Trapani where they arrived at 0800 B/28.
At 1200 B/27, in position 38°22'N, 15°39'E, a destroyer was sighted bearing 270° at a range of 4 miles. Enemy course was 100°.
This was most probably the torpedo boat San Martino which sailed from Messina at 0925 B/27 to search for survivors of the Albatros and returned to Messina at 1245 B/27.

At 1630 B/27, in position 38°17'N, 15°42'E, a hospital ship, thought to be the Virgilio (Italian, 11718 GRT, built 1928) bearing 180° at a range of 4 miles. Her course was 000° but at 1645 B/27, she changed course to 270°. Aircraft appeared to be escorting the hospital ship.
This was indeed the Virgilio which had sailed from Augusta at 1045 B/27 for Palermo and to search for survivors of the Albatros and of an aircraft which had been lost off Palermo.
At 1725 B/27, in position 38°20'N, 15°45'E, a three-funnel torpedo boat was sighted bearing 180° at a range of 4 miles. Enemy course was 350° but 10 minutes later she altered course 270°.
This was most probably the torpedo boat Generale Antonino Cascino which sailed from Messina at 1620 B/27 for an A/S search and returned at 0845 B/28. (19)

28 Sep 1941 At 0930 B/28, in position 38°22'N, 15°43'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) sighted a patrol vessel and a minesweeper bearing 160° at a range of 4 miles. Enemy course was 000°. We have been unable to identify these vessels.
At 1015 B/28, in position 38°22'N, 15°38'E, sighted three minesweepers bearing 200° at a range of 5 miles. Enemy course was 270°. We have been unable to identify these vessels.

At 1131 B/28, in position 38°19'N, 15°45'E, sighted what is thought to be a motor torpedo boat moving north-eastward in short burst and stopping around every quarter mile presumably to listen on hydrophones. Lt.Cdr. Cayley decided to retire to the north shortly afterwards. We have been unable to identify this vessel.
At 1300 B/28, in position 38°22'N, 15°38'E, sighted three minesweepers bearing 235° at a range of 5 miles. Enemy course was 100°. We have been unable to identify these vessels.
At 1325 B/28, in position 38°29'N, 15°49'E, sighted two minesweepers bearing 030° at a range of 5 miles. Enemy course was 190°. We have been unable to identify these vessels.
At 2015 B/28, in position 38°39'N, 15°43'E, sighted a destroyer bearing 210° at a range of only half a mile. Enemy course was 020°.
This was most probably the torpedo boat Rosolino Pilo which sailed from Messina at 1930 B/28 for an A/S sweep and returned at 1320 B/29. (19)

29 Sep 1941 At 0001 B/29, in position 38°42'N, 15°46'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) sighted what is thought to be a motor torpedo boat bearing 075° at a range of 2 miles. Enemy course was 330°. Utmost dived. HE indicated that the enemy was circling in the vicinity for about an hour.
At 0400 B/29, HMS Utmost surfaced to complete the battery charge which was completed half an hour later.
At 0434 B/29, having just completed the charged HE as of a motor torpedo boat was picked up on bearing 100° and closing. Utmost dived. The HE was heard for a few more minutes before it disappeared.

At 1600 B/29, in position 38°21'N, 15°40'E, the Vichy-French hospital ship Canada (9684 GRT, built 1912) was seen bearing 180° at a range of 7 miles. Her course was 270°. A flying boat appeared to be escorting her.
At 2005 B/29, HMS Utmost left area 'W' to proceed to the Marittimo area. (19)

30 Sep 1941 At 1320 B/30, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) received a signal from Capt. S.10 timed 1140 B/30 which informed her that three northbound merchant ships speed 10 knots expected to pass through position 38°00'N, 12°00'E on course 30° between 0040 B/2 and 0240 B/2. Endeavour to intercept before making passage to south-eastwards to Malta.
In a signal timed 1945 B/30 the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Utmost that Swordfish aircraft would attack this convoy that night.

This signal referred to the convoy made up of the transports Caterina (Italian, 4838 GRT, built 1920), Marin Sanudo (Italian, 5958 GRT, built 1926) and the tanker Minatitland (Italian, 7651 GRT, built 1941) escorted by destroyers Alpino, Alfredo Oriani and Strale which was on passage from Tripoli to Naples. (24)

1 Oct 1941 At 0538 B/1, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) heard HE (140 revs) of two ships bearing 140° and passing to westward. Nothing could be seen due to the poor visibility. Also the contacts were up against the land.
At 0615 B/1, high speed HE picked up moving eastwards. It sounded like a motor torpedo boat.
At 0645 B/1, north-west of Cape St. Vito, in position 38°13'N, 12°41'E, a motor torpedo boat was sighted bearing 210° at a range of 1.5 miles proceeding on course 050° at 30 knots.

In a signal timed 1417 B/1, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Utmost that three merchant ships and two destroyers had been reported at 1015 B/1 proceeding on course 000° in position 35°35'N, 12°28'E (north-west of Lampedusa). (19)

2 Oct 1941 HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) attacked an enemy convoy off Marettimo Island. Only one torpedo was fired instead of the intended three as Utmost was detected by the escorts and the attack had to be broken off.

The convoy attacked was made up of the transports Caterina (Italian, 4838 GRT, built 1920), Marin Sanudo (Italian, 5958 GRT, built 1926) and the tanker Minatitland (Italian, 7651 GRT, built 1941) escorted by destroyers Alpino, Alfredo Oriani and Strale proceeding from Tripoli to Naples. It was the Oriani who sighted the surfaced submarine and rushed to the attack, dropping 22 depth charges after the submarine had dived. This convoy had sailed from Tripoli at 1430-1600 B/30 and arrived in Naples at 0130-0300 B/3.

0119 B/2 - In position 37°53'N, 12°05'E sighted a convoy bearing 130°, range about 6 nautical miles, enemy course 330°. Escort a provided by two destroyers but most likely a third was astern. Attack conditions were very difficult. An attack was started and one torpedo was fired at 0142 B/2. More torpedoes were intended but a green Very light was fired in the direction of Utmost by an unseen destroyer. Utmost had to no other choice then to dive at 0143 B/2. The torpedo that was fired was heard to explode and is thought to have hit the target. Following this attack 14 depth charges were dropped by the escorts but none were close and no damage was sustained. (5)
3 Oct 1941 Around 1300 B/3, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) ended her 14th war patrol (12th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (5)
11 Oct 1941 HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) is docked down in No.2 graving dock at Malta. She had entered the dock the day before. (25)
15 Oct 1941 HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) is undocked. (25)
21 Oct 1941 HMS Utmost (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) and ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B. Karnicki) conducted exercises off Malta with HMS Abingdon (Lt. G.A. Simmers, RNR). (25)

23 Oct 1941 Around 1710 A/23, HMS Utmost (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) departed Malta for 15th war patrol (13th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off Kuriat and also to perform a special operation. Lt. J.D. Martin, RN, had taken over command for this patrol from Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN in order to give this last officer a rest and to enable complete recovery from sandfly fever.

25 Oct 1941 At 1115 A/25, in position 36°28'N, 10°50'E, HMS Utmost (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) sighted a ship bearing 030° at a range of 8 miles. The ship was found to be aground with an armed tug alongside on the port side and a trawler on the other side. The ship was identified as the Vichy-French Ostrevent (1737 GRT, built 1925). The ship had French colours painted on her side for and aft and all three vessels flew French ensigns. (19)
26 Oct 1941 At 0245 A/26, HMS Utmost (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) completed her special operation in the Gulf of Hammamet. The Folbot sank as it was returning but the crew managed to swim to the submarine.

This was probably to land stores for the remnants of the Breuillac resistance network. After sabotaging a couple of Italian ships, one of the saboteurs was captured and talked. Mounier had been forced to flee to Malta but was killed on his return trip when his plane (a captured Heinkel 115) crashed.
On completion of the special operation HMS Utmost proceeded to patrol off the Gulf of Hammamet in accordance with Capt. S.10's signal timed 1422 A/22.
At 1000 A/26, the Vichy-French merchant vessel Ostrevent (1737 GRT, built 1925) was seen in tow of two tugs. She was close inshore near Hammamet.
At 2345 A/26, two lighted trawlers were seen to the east-north-east off Kuriat Island in position 35°49'N, 11°10'E. (19)
27 Oct 1941 At 0050 A/27, HMS Utmost (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) entered area 'K' (35°18'N, 11°11'E, 345° - 30 - 60.

At 1530 A/27, in position 35°39'N, 11°06'E, a Vichy-French destroyer of the Bourrasque-class was seen. She passed 3000 yards inshore. She was northbound at 16 knots. The vessel was displaying penant number T 73. (19)

30 Oct 1941 At 1050 A/30, HMS Utmost (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) received Capt. S.10's signal timed 0947 A/30 which stated a tanker escorted by a destroyer had been seen at 0740 A/30 in position 34°32'N, 11°10'E, proceeding on course 070° at 8 knots, They will probably hug the coast.

In a signal timed 1310 A/30, Capt. S.10 gave the following instructions to HMS Utmost; Leave patrol as requested to proceed through positions 35°42'N, 12°55'E and 35°42'N, 14°10'E so as to arrive Marsamxett 0630 A/3.

At 1635 A/30, while running on the surface to the west-north-west of Lampedusa, in position 35°43'N, 12°02'E, HMS Utmost was strafed and straddled by machine gun bullets from an aircraft. The aircraft approached down sun and was never seen before HMS Utmost crash dived. (26)

31 Oct 1941 HMS Utmost (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN), off Kutiat Island, in position 35°47'N, 11°04'E, fired one torpedo at the grounded Italian merchant vessel Marigola (5996 GRT, built 1906). The torpedo missed though.

2300 A/30 - Sighted a merchant vessel in position 35°47'N, 11°04'E. The vessel was thought to be of around 4500 GRT. At first it was thought the ship was at anchor or rendezvousing. After the range had closed to 500 yards it was seen that the vessel was aground. She appeared intact and undamaged. Decided to fire a torpedo at it.

0020 A/31 - Fired one torpedo but nothing was heard or seen from this torpedo and it is considered it struck bottom before it had run off its safety range. Depth on firing was 6 fathoms. After firing withdrew to seawards.

1835 A/31 - During daylight the stranded vessel had been carefully observed through the periscope. She was painted grey overall and had a gun of about 4" calibre on the stern. The vessel appeared laden and had no list or inclination fore and aft. Her boats were lowered on either side just clear of the water. A motor lorry was suspended from a derrick just forward of the bridge. Smoke was issuing from the funnel during the day and steam was seen escaping as from winches. Considered salvage work was in progress and decided to attack with the gun during darkness.

2350 A/31 - In position 35°51'N, 10°52'E, sighted two lighted trawlers bearing 260° at a range of 15 miles. Course was 090°. As the position of these vressels made an approach to the stranded vessel, while remaining unseen, impossible it was decided to defer the gun action until the following night. (19)
1 Nov 1941 HMS Utmost (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) destroys the (already grounded and damaged) Italian merchant vessel Marigola (5996 GRT, built 1906) off Kuriat Island, Tunisia.

2146 A/1 - In position 35°47'N, 11°05'E, sighted a vessel bearing 190° at a range of 8 miles proceeding on a northerly course. Gave chase and identified the vessel as a small coaster of about 500 tons. Decided to let it go and thy to destroy the much larger, stranded ship with gunfire.
2230 A/1 - Set course and speed to close the stranded vessel.
2303 A/1 - Opened fire on this vessel. In all 39 HE and 19 SAP shells were fired from 400 yards. About 50 hits were obtained.
2335 A/1 - Retired to seaward. The stranded vessel was now definitely a total loss. (19)
2 Nov 1941 At 1200 A/2, HMS Utmost (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) left patrol to return to Malta. (19)
3 Nov 1941 Around 0615 A/3, HMS Utmost (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) ended her 15th war patrol (13th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (19)

15 Nov 1941 Around 1630 B/15, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) departed Malta for her 16th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean). She was initially ordered to patrol off the west coast of Greece in area 'M', a rectangular area bounded by lines between the following positions; 37°39'N, 19°44'E, 37°46'N, 19°32'E, 38°24'N, 20°13'E and 38°17'N, 20°25'E.
On departure from Malta practice attacks were carried out on HMS Abingdon (Lt. G.A. Simmers, RNR). (19)
15 Nov 1941 For the daily and attack positions of HMS Utmost during her 16th war patrol see the map below.

16 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1221 B/16, Capt. S.10, ordered HMS Thunderbolt (Lt.Cdr. C.B. Crouch, DSO, RN) to patrol in position 37°58'N, 15°34'E and HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) to patrol in position 37°51'N, 15°43'E. Both positions were to the south of the Straits of Messina. An important 12 to 14 knots south-east bound convoy probably heavily escorted may be expected late afternoon 17/11. The object of attack are the merchant ships of the convoy. Further instructions are to follow. (27)

16 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1635 B/16, the Capt. S.10 send the following orders to the following submarines; HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) proceed as ordered in the signal timed 1221 B/16. HMS Thunderbolt (Lt. C.B. Crouch, DSO, RN) proceed towards Cephalonia. HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) and HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) are to remain on patrol. Further instructions will follow. (27)

18 Nov 1941 At 1020 B/18, south of the Straits of Messina, in position 37°52'N, 15°32'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) sighted a hospital ship, thought to be either Sicilia (Italian, 9646 GRT, built 1924) or Toscana (Italian, 9429 GRT, built 1923), bearing 280° at a range of 10 miles. The hospital ship's course was 200°.
This was the Italian Virgilio (11718 GRT, built 1926) which sailed from Naples at 1845 B/17 for Augusta where she arrived at 1400 B/18.
At 1704 B/18, in position 37°53'N, 15°36'E, a motor torpedo boat was sighted bearing 310° at a range of 2 miles. Enemy course was 360°. We have been unable to identify this vessel. (5)

20 Nov 1941 At 1155 B/20, south of the Straits of Messina, in position 37°51'N, 15°33'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) sighted a convoy of three merchant vessels and three destroyers with a flying boat as air escort overhead, bearing 260° at a range of 10 miles. Enemy course was 160° at 12 knots.
At 1200 B/20, HMS Utmost altered course to 180° with the object of intercepting the convoy should the turn to the east.
At 1220 B/20, the convoy altered course to 135°. Ran in at high speed on a 140° track, but broke off the attack at 1440 B/20 when it became obvious that convoy would pass well out of range.
At 1300 B/20, HMS Utmost commenced to pass and enemy report but found too many aircraft in the vicinity so the attempt had to be postponed.
At 1330 B/20, the same convoy was seen again bearing 100° at a range of 10 miles now on course 020° presumably heading for Taranto.

This convoy made up of the transports Sebastiano Venier (Italian, 6311 GRT, built 1940) and Ankara (German, 4768 GRT, built 1937) escorted by the destroyers Alfredo Oriani and Vincenzo Gioberti had sailed from Naples at 1758-2000 B/19 for Tripoli via Argostoli. The destroyer Maestrale, which had been delayed and sailed at 0005 B/20 and joined the convoy at 0715 B/20. The convoy arrived at Argostoli at 0905-1010 B/21. They sailed again at 0445 B/22 and arrived at Taranto at 1930 B/22. Eventually, Sebastiano Venier sailed for Benghazi on 29 November and Ankara sailed for the same destination in another convoy only on 16 December 1941.
At 1615 B/20, a schooner was seen coast crawling off Cape Pellara. (5)
21 Nov 1941 HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) torpedoed and damaged the Italian heavy cruiser Trieste south of the Straits of Messina in position 37°48'N, 15°32'E.

The convoy the cruisers were escorting was in two sections: The transports Napoli (Italian, 6142 GRT, built 1941) and Vettor Pisani (Italian, 6339 GRT, built 1939) were screened by the destroyer Turbine and the torpedo boat Perseo. The transports Iridio Mantovani (Italian, 10540 GRT, built 1939) and Monginevro (Italian, 5324 GRT, built 1940) were screened by the destroyer Nicoloso Da Recco and the torpedo boat Enrico Cosenz. They all had sailed from Naples 1758 B/20-0513 B/21. The Eighth Cruiser Squadron, made up of the Giuseppe Garibaldi and Luigi di Savoia Duca Degli Abruzzi screened by the destroyers Granatiere, Bersagliere and Alpino sailed from Naples at 0530 B-0750/21. It caught up with the convoy at 1630 B/21. The 14th Destroyer Squadron, made up of the Ugolino Vivaldi, Emanuele Pessagno and Antonio da Noli sailed from Reggio Calabria at 1318-1600 B/21 to join the escort. The Third Cruiser Division, made up of the Gorizia, Trieste and Trento, screened by the destroyers Aviere, Geniere, Camicia Nera, Corazziere and Carabiniere sailed from Messina at 1735-1930 B/21 and joined up at 1946 B/21. The convoy transited the Straits of Messina at about 2000 B/21. The destroyer Nicoloso da Recco and the torpedo boat Enrico Cosenz were detached and returned to Naples where they arrived at 0430-0522 B/22. At 2312 B/21, the heavy cruiser Trieste was torpedoed by HMS Utmost. At 0038 B/22, the light cruiser Giuseppe Garibaldi was torpedoed by an enemy bomber. Trieste, Giuseppe Garibaldi and Luigi di Savoia Duca Degli Abruzzi screened by the destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Antonio da Noli, Granatiere, Bersagliere, Alpino and the torpedo boat Perseo proceeded to Messina where they arrived at 0745-1340 B/22. Trieste had been hit in boiler no.3 and had 22 killed and three wounded and temporarily immobilised but at 0038 B/22 hours managed to proceed. It was estimated that she had embarked 3000 tons of water. She was out of action until mid-July 1942. The destroyers Corazziere, Carabiniere and Turbine proceeded to Reggio Calabria where they arrived at 1145-1330 B/22. The convoy was diverted to Taranto. Napoli, Vettor Pisani, Iridio Mantovani, Gorizia and Trento screened by the destroyers Aviere, Geniere, Camicia Nera, arrived at Taranto at 1717 B/22-0113 B/23. Monginevro escorted by the destroyer Emanuele Pessagno arrived at Taranto at 0404 B/23.

1000 B/21 - In position 37°42'N, 15°41'E, a three-funnel torpedo boat was seen bearing 180° at a range of 8 miles. Enemy course was 070°. This was most probably the torpedo boat Giuseppe Dezza which sailed from Messina at 0700 B/21 for an A/S sweep off Cape Passero.
At 1045 B/21 - In position 37°53'N, 15°40'E, a small patrol vessel was seen bearing 305° at a range of 2 miles. The enemy was proceeding eastwards along the coast.
At 1410 B/21 - In position 37°48'N, 15°50'E, sighted a patrol vessel bearing 120° at a range of 8 miles. Enemy course was 230°.

At 1700 B/21 - In position 37°48'N, 15°41'E, what is thought to be a motor torpedo boat is sighted bearing 200° at a range of 2 miles. Enemy course was 100°. This was most probably either MAS 547 or MAS 562 which had sailed from Messina at 0800 B/21 for an A/S sweep and returned at 1935 B/21.

2300 B/21 - In position 37°48'N, 15°32'E heard HE. Shortly afterwards sighted three Italian cruisers and three destroyers bearing 275°, range about 5 nautical miles, enemy course 110°, speed 20 knots. Started attack in which four torpedoes were fired at the rear cruiser.

2312 B/21 - One hit was obtained just abaft the fore funnel. A column of flame rose over 200 feet in the air and the sea was illuminated for considerable distance so dived. Two depth charges were dropped shortly afterwards. Utmost meanwhile retired to the south-east.
2355 B/21 - A rumbling noise was heard. It was hoped that this was the cruiser sinking. A further 84 depth charges were dropped but by now Utmost was well clear. (19)

21 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1620 B/21, Capt. S.10 informed HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) that two convoys were approaching Messina from the north-west. Time of sighting was 1410 B/21. One made up of two merchant ships and six destroyers and another convoy of two merchant vessels escorted by two torpedo boats further to the north of the first one sighted. (27)
22 Nov 1941 At 0430 B/22, in position 37°36'N, 16°05'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) sighted a hospital ship bearing 220° at a range of 7 miles on course 360°.
The Italian hospital ship Virgilio (11718 GRT, built 1928) was in the area and arrived at Augusta at 1550 B/22 from Naples but she should have been proceeding to the southward.
At 1125 B/22, in position 37°37'N, 15°41'E, a destroyer was sighted bearing 307° at a range of 8 miles. Enemy course was 040°. The destroyer altered course to 280° at 1220 B/22.
Perhaps this was the torpedo boat Giuseppe Dezza which had sailed from Messina at 0700 B/21 for an A/S search and returned Messina at 1740 B/22. (5)
24 Nov 1941 At 0300 B/24, in position 37°40'N, 15°47'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) sighted a hospital ship bearing 000° at a range of 5 miles on course 135°.

This was most probably the Arno (Italian, 8024 GRT, built 1912) which had sailed from Naples at 1120 B/23 for Benghazi via Messina. She arrived at Benghazi before noon on 25 November. She sailed from Benghazi to return to Italy with wounded at 1530 B/25.
At 1120 B/24, in position 37°45'N, 15°48'E, sighted a schooner bearing 190° at a range of 3 miles. Enemy course was 020°. (5)
25 Nov 1941 At 1200 B/25, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) left patrol to return to Malta. (5)
27 Nov 1941 Around 0720 B/27, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) ended her 16th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (19)

9 Dec 1941 Around 1755 B/9, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) departed Malta for her 17th war patrol (15th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the Gulf of Taranto in position 39°33'N, 17°39'E in the centre of a patrol line. HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) and HMS Unbeaten (Lt. Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) being 8 miles away in a west-south-west and east-north-east direction respectively.

9 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 1928 B/9, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN), HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN), HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) were ordered that no northbound merchant ships of more than 2000 tons and less than 10000 tons whether escorted or not is to be attacked by you from receipt of this message until noon 11/l2 unless it is definitely identified as a tanker.
The above signal was cancelled in Capt. S.10's signal timed 0104 B/10. (28)
11 Dec 1941 At 1235 B/11, in position 38°28'N, 17°05'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) sighted two destroyers bearing 245° at a range of 9 miles. Enemy course was 020° at 22 knots.

These were Lanzerotto Malocello and Saetta. Initially, the destroyers Lanzerotto Malocello and Antoniotto Usodimare had sailed from Trapani at 1645-1737 B/10, escorting the transport Carlo Del Greco (Italian, 6837 GRT, built 1941) to Messina. Later they were joined by the Saetta. At 0745 B/11, Malocello and Saetta left them off Messina and continued to Taranto where they arrived at 2100-2146 B/11. (19)

11 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 0924 B/11, Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Unbeaten (Lt. Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN), HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN), HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) to form R line as follows: 3 - 39°27'N, 17°24'E - 060° - 13 - 150° - 20. Position A (39°27'N, 17°24'E), HMS Upright, Position B, HMS Utmost and position C, HMS Unbeaten. (28)

12 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 1034 B/12, Capt. S.10 gave the following orders to HMS Unbeaten (Lt. Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN), HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN), HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN);

Proceed forthwith to establish patrol during daylight hours on 13 December as follows: Upright in position 40°08'N, 17°00'E, Utmost in position 40°00'N, 17°11'E, Unbeaten in position 40°12'N, 17°19'E. Attention is called to QB 4 (an Italian minefield) Heavy units may leave Taranto A.M./13 proceeding south. After attack or at 1930 B/13 withdraw to original patrol as in my signal 0924 B/11.

In a signal timed 1615 B/12, the Capt. S.10 infored the above submarines that two merchant vessels and three destroyers had been spotted at 1505 B/12 in position 38°00'N, 17°00'E proceeding on course 030° at 10 knots. (28)

13 Dec 1941 HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) attacked the Italian transports Fabio Filzi (6836 GRT, built 1940) and Carlo del Greco (6837 GRT, built 1941) in the Gulf of Taranto in position 39°47'N, 17°22'E. All four torpedoes fired missed their targets. The convoy, which was on passage from Messina to Taranto, was escorted by the Italian destroyers Nicoloso da Recco and Antoniotto Usodimare.

At 1510 B/13, the masts and funnels of a merchant vessel escorted by two destroyers were sighted. This must have been the German transport Ankara (4768 GRT, built 1937) escorted by the destroyers Lanzerotto Malocello and Saetta. This convoy sailed from Taranto at 1255 B/13 from Taranto for Benghazi (see below).

There were many movements during the afternoon and evening as three vital convoys to Libya sailed (two from Taranto, one from Argostoli). The destroyer Emanuele Pessagno [part of the screen of the Monginevro convoy, see below] sighted a submarine, probably HMS Utmost at 2245 B/13 in 180° - Torre dell’Ovo [Taranto] – 41 miles [or about 39°35'N, 17°31'E].

Convoy 1 was made up of the transports Monginevro (Italian 5324 GRT, built 1940) and Vettor Pisani (Italian 6339 GRT, built 1939) screened by the destroyers Emanuele Pessagno, Antoniotto Usodimare and the torpedo boat Pegaso sailed from Taranto at 1445-1700 B/13 and returned to Taranto at 0625-0635 B/14. Cover for this convoy was provided by the battleship Caio Duilio, the heavy cruiser Gorizia, the light cruisers Giuseppe Garibaldi, Eugenio Montecuccoli screened by the destroyers Maestrale, Alfredo Oriani, Vincenzo Gioberti, Nicolò Zeno, Corazziere and Carabiniere.

Convoy 2 was made up of the transport Ankara (German 4768 GRT, built 1937) screened by the destroyer Lanzerotto Malocello had sailed from Taranto at 1045 B/13 for Benghazi, at about 12-12.5 knots, on course 135°. The destroyer Saetta sailed from Taranto at 1158 B/13 and joined them at 1355 B/13. At 0200 B/14, they were ordered back and arrived at Taranto at 1757-2129 B/14. Cover for this convoy was provided by the battleship Andrea Doria, the light cruisers Emanuele Filiberto Duca d’Aosta and Muzio Attendolo screened by the destroyers Aviere, Geniere, Camicia Nera, Ugolino Vivaldi, Antonio Da Noli and Nicoloso Da Recco which sailed from Taranto at 1754-2042 B/13.

Convoy 3 was made up of the transports Iseo (Italian, 2366 GRT, built 1918) and Capo Orso (Italian, 3149 GRT, built 1916) screened by the destroyers Turbine sailed from Argostoli at 1745 B/12, the destroyer Strale sailed at 1530 B/13 to join them. They returned at 1155-1212 B/14.

Distant cover for the three convoys was provided by the battleships Littorio and Vittorio Veneto screened by the destroyers Granatiere, Fuciliere, Bersagliere and Alpino sailed from Naples at 1620-1840 B/13 [HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) later torpedoed and damaged the Vittorio Veneto]. The torpedo boats Clio and Centauro sailed from Messina at 0730-0734 B/14 to reinforce the escort while the torpedo boat Giuseppe Dezza sailed from Messina at 1400 B/13 for an A/S sweep.
The three convoys were ordered to turn back and were back in port on 14 December.
Shortly after this attack, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) had better luck and sent both transports to the bottom of the sea.
0050 B/13 - Heard HE bearing 135°.
0110 B/13 - In position 39°47'N, 17°22'E sighted two destroyers and two merchant vessels bearing 130°, range about 6 nautical miles, enemy course 330°, speed 15 knots. Started attack.
0132 B/13 - Fired four torpedoes, two at each merchant ship. The nearest target was then at 5000 yards range.
0133 B/13 - Dived and retired to the South.
0139 B/13 - Heard one torpedo hit.
0150 B/13 - Depth charging started. About 40 were dropped throughout the night.
1350 B/13 - Heard HE bearing 310°.
1355 B/13 - In position 39°41'N, 17°21'E, sighted two patrol vessels bearing 315° at a range of 4 miles. Enemy course was 090°.

1510 B/13 - In position 39°44'N, 17°30'E, masts and funnels of a merchant vessel escorted by two destroyers were seen bearing 030° at a range of 7 miles. Enemy course was 130°. Three aircraft were patrolling overhead this convoy. HMS Utmost altered course towards but the attack was soon broken off as it was realised it was not possible to get within a reasonable attacking range.
1820 B/13 - Picked up destroyer HE bearing 038°. Nothing was sighted though.
1925 B/13 - In position 39°41'N, 17°22'E, two patrol vessels were sighted bearing 190° at a range of 2 miles. Enemy course was 330°. HMS Utmost manoeuvred round these to the southward.
Between 2000 B/13 and 2300 B/13 - HE of a number of destroyers was heard in various directions. None passed close enough to be visible.
2330 B/13 - Proceeded northwards to take up position 40°15'N, 17°10'E by dawn. (19)

13 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 2037 B/13, Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Unbeaten (Lt. Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN), HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) to remain in position ordered as in his signal timed 1034 B/12, until attack or 1000 B/14 then returning to the original patrol line as ordered in his signal timed 0924 B/11. Big game was to be expected. (28)
14 Dec 1941 At 0125 B/14, in position 39°50'N, 17°30'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) sighted a destroyer bearing 080° at a range of 4 miles. Enemy course was 360° at 18 knots.
Perhaps this was the Carabiniere which arrived at 0347 B/14 (see list below of arrivals at Taranto).

At 0355 B/14, in position 40°02'N, 17°22'E, a large merchant vessel and two destroyers were seen bearing 045° at a range of 6 miles. Enemy course was 335°. Utmost ran in to attack on a 110° track. Lt.Cdr. Cayley took into consideration the possible departure of heavy units from Taranto and also the meagre chance of hitting this particular target due to the long range. He therefore decided to break off the attack.

This were most probably the transports Monginevro (Italian, 5324 GRT, built 1940) and Vettor Pisani (Italian, 6339 GRT, built 1939) screened by the destroyers Emanuele Pessagno and Antoniotto Usodimare which arrived at Taranto at 0625 B-0635 B/14.
At 1030 B/14, in position 40°20'N, 17°12'E, two patrol vessels were sighted bearing 030° at a range of 4 miles. Enemy course was 215°. We have been unable to identify these vessels.

At 1145 B/14, in position 40°17'N, 17°04'E, what is thought to be a Spica-class torpedo boat was seen bearing 270° at a range of 3 miles. Enemy course was 010° at a speed of 20 knots. Two aircraft were patrolling over this torpedo boat.
This may have been the torpedo boat Pegaso which arrived at Taranto at 1330 B/14.
At 1325 B/14, HE was picked up.
At 1330 B/14, in position 40°16'N, 17°07'E, three destroyers were sighted bearing 320° at a range of 4 miles proceeding on course 210°.
This were most likely the Ugolino Vivaldi, Antonio da Noli and Carabiniere which had sailed from Taranto at 1200-1215 B/14 to reinforce the escort of the Vittorio Veneto squadron.
At 1347 B/14, three more destroyers were sighted on bearing 330°.
This were most likely the Aviere, Geniere and Camicia Nera which had sailed from Taranto at 1245-1325 B/14 to reinforce the escort of the Vittorio Veneto squadron
At 1349 B/14, the first group of three destroyers altered course to 160°.
At 1405 B/14, the second group of three destroyers altered course to 170°.
At 1515 B/14, in position 40°00'N, 17°03'E, a merchant vessel escorted by a destroyer and two aircraft were sighted bearing 240° at a range of 6 miles. Enemy course was 330° at 10 knots.

This was the transport Ankara (German, 4768 GRT, built 1937) escorted by the destroyers Lanzerotto Malocello and Saetta which arrived at Taranto at 1757 B/14. Malocello arrived at 2129 B/14 due to a steering breakdown near the entrance to Taranto.
At 1620 B/14, in position 39°59'N, 17°10'E, two Spica-class torpedo boats were sighted bearing 295° at a range of 3 miles. enemy course was 165°.
There were most probably the torpedo boats Lince and Aretusa which sailed from Taranto at 1430 B/14 for an A/S sweep and returned at 2210-2310 B/14.
At 1700 B/14, HMS Utmost was forced deep by an aircraft thus missing the 1700 routine (incoming signals were to expected around 1700 hours).
At 1830 B/14, HMS Utmost surfaced.

At 2035 B/14, In position 39°50'N, 17°26'E, sighted two destroyers. Dived between them. The HE of more destroyers was picked up, all averaging about 220 revs. Distant HE of two ships doing 160 revs was also heard. These may have been the battleships reported in Capt. S.10's signal timed 1535 B/14 which was not received by HMS Utmost until 2126 B/14 due to missing the 1700 hours routine. The enemy was proceeding northwards, obviously returning to Taranto.

These destroyers most probably belonged to the Littorio Squadron made up of the battleships Littorio and Vittorio Veneto, screened by the destroyers Fuciliere, Granatiere, Alpino, Maestrale, Bersagliere, Corazziere, Vincenzo Gioberti, Ugolino Vivaldi, Antonio da Noli, Carabiniere, Aviere, Alfredo Oriani, Geniere and Camicia Nera proceeding to Taranto where they arrived at 2246 B/14-0135 B/15.
At 2200 B/14, HMS Utmost surfaced.
At 2230 B/14, HMS Utmost attempted to pass her 2227 B/14 to Malta W/T but the W/T set was found to be defective. After repairs the message was finally passed at 0245 B/15.
At 2355 B/14, course was set to establish the patrol line in the original position across the entrance to the Gulf of Taranto.
Arrivals at Taranto of Italian warships were as follows; Battleships: Vittorio Veneto 2340 B/14 Littorio 2342 B/14 Andrea Doria 0157 B/14 Caio Duilio 0250 B/14

Cruisers: Gorizia 0200 B/14? Destroyers: Emanuele Pessagno 0625 B/14 (escorting Monginevro and Vettor Pisani) Antoniotto Usodimare 0635 B/14 (escorting Monginevro and Vettor Pisani) Maestrale 0039 B/15 (escorting the Vittorio B/14 off Taranto due to steering broke down) Saetta 1757 B/14 (escorting Ankara) Ugolino Vivaldi 0024 B/14, (with Doria and Duilio squadron) Sailed again at 1204 B/14 to join the Vittorio Veneto and 0240 B/14 (with Doria and Duilio squadron) Sailed again at 1250 B/14 to join the Vittorio Veneto and Littorio squadron at 1636 B/14 She returned at 2305 B/14 Geniere 0235 B/14 (with Doria and Duilio squadron) Sailed again at 1325 B/14 to join the Vittorio Veneto and Littorio squadron at 1636 B/14 She returned at 2315 B/14 Camicia Nera 0308 B/14(with Doria and Duilio squadron) Sailed again at 1245 B/14 to join the Vittorio Veneto and Littorio squadron at 1636 B/14 She returned at 0026 B/15 Nicoloso Da Recco 0200 B/14 (with Doria and Duilio squadron) Granatiere 2314 B/14 (escorting the Vittorio (escorting the Vittorio Veneto and Littorio squadron)

Torpedo boats: Antares 0915 B/14 (from Navarino) Pegaso 1330 B/14 (escorting Monginevro and Vettor Pisani) Lince 2210 B/14 (sailed from Taranto at 1430 B/14 for an A/S sweep) Aretusa 2310 B/14 (sailed from Taranto at 1430 B/14 for an A/S sweep) (19)

15 Dec 1941 At 1130 B/15, in position 39°39'N, 17°48'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) sighted a Navigatori-class destroyer bearing 030° at a range of 8 miles. Enemy course was 320° at 20 knots.
This was the Italian destroyer Antonio Pigafetta which had sailed from Taranto at 0855 B/15, carrying petrol for the Afrikakorps and reached Benghazi at 1130 B/16. (19)
Operation MF 1, passage of the British supply ship HMS Breconshire to Malta.

Around 2200 B/15 the British supply ship HMS Breconshire (9776 GRT, built 1939) departed Alexandria being escorted by HMS Naiad (Capt. M.A.H. Kelsey, DSC, RN flying the flag of Rear-Admiral P.L. Vian, DSO and 2 Bars, RN), HMS Euryalus (Capt. E.W. Bush, DSO, DSC, RN), HMS Carlisle (Capt. D.M.L. Neame, DSO, RN), HMS Jervis (Capt. P.J. Mack, DSO and Bar, RN), HMS Kimberley (Cdr. J.S.M. Richardson, DSO, RN), HMS Kingston (Lt.Cdr. P. Somerville, DSO, DSC, RN), HMS Kipling (Cdr. A. St.Clair Ford, RN), HMAS Nizam (Lt.Cdr. M.J. Clark, RAN), HMS Havock (Lt. G.R.G. Watkins, DSC, RN) and HMS Decoy (Lt.Cdr. J.M. Alliston, RN). This last destroyer was also to proceed to Malta for repairs to her bow that had been damaged in a collision at Alexandria on 4 December. HMS Breconshire was carrying oil fuel for Malta.

At 1100/16 the Allied destroyers HMS Sikh (Cdr. G.H. Stokes, DSC, RN), HMS Maori (Cdr. R.E. Courage, DSO, DSC and Bar, RN), HMS Legion (Cdr. R.F. Jessel, RN) and HrMs Isaac Sweers (Cdr. J. Houtsmuller, RNN) departed Malta. They joined up with the convoy at daylight on the 17th. During the day the convoy was attacked by enemy high level and torpedo bombers

These were followed at 1800 B/16 by ‘Force K’; HMS Aurora (Capt. W.G. Agnew, CB, RN), HMS Penelope (Capt. A.D. Nicholl, RN), HMS Lance (Lt.Cdr. R.W.F. Northcott, RN) and HMS Lively (Lt.Cdr. W.F.E. Hussey, DSC, RN).

At dark on the 16th HMS Carlisle, HMS Havock and HMS Kingston were detached to make a W/T diversion to the eastward at midnight of the night of 16/17 and then to proceed to Alexandria. They were later joined by HMS Griffin (Capt. H.St.L. Nicolson, DSO and Bar, RN).

Enemy heavy forces were reported at sea at 2230 B/16 by the submarines HMS Unbeaten (Lt. Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) in the Gulf of Taranto area. Neither submarine was able to attack. The Italians were at sea to cover an imported Axis convoy to North Africa.

From Taranto had departed the transports Monginevro (5324 GRT, built 1940), Napoli (6142 GRT, built 1941) and Vettor Pisani (6339 GRT, built 1939). They had a close escort of the destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Antonio Da Noli, Nicoloso da Recco, Lanzerotto Malocello, Emanuele Pessagno, Nicolò Zeno. From Naples the German transport Ankara (4768 GRT, built 1937) departed on the same day. She had a close escort made up of the destroyer Saetta and the torpedo-boat Pegaso.

Cover was provided by two groups of warships. One group was made up of the battleship Caio Dulio, the light cruisers Emanuele Filiberto Duca D’Aosta, Muzio Attendolo, Raimondo Montecuccoli and the destroyers Aviere, Ascari and Camicia Nera. The other, and larger group, was made up of the battleships Littorio, Andrea Doria, Guilio Cesare, heavy cruisers Gorizia, Trento and the destroyers Granatiere, Bersagliere, Fuciliere, Alpino, Corazziere, Carabiniere, Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale, Alfredo Oriani and Vincenzo Gioberti.

The enemy heavy forces were reported by reconnaissance aircraft at 0825 B/17 and again at 1525 B/17 when they were with their convoy and only about 60 nautical miles from the Allied convoy. Very few Allied aircraft were available for reconnaissance and shadowing was therefore not carried out at all. At 1745 B/17 the Allied convoy unexpectedly ran into the larger of the Italian cover forces. The Italian battleships opened fire but drew off to the northward when the Allied convoy escorts closed to attack. Contact was lost in the dark. When both forces made contact HMS Breconshire was detached with HMS Havock and HMS Decoy as escorts. They later made rendez-vous with ‘Force K’.
To reinforce the convoy HMS Neptune (Capt. R.C. O’Coner, RN), HMS Jaguar (Lt.Cdr. L.R.K. Tyrwhitt, DSC, RN) and HMS Kandahar (Cdr. W.G.A. Robson, DSO, DSC, RN) were sailed from Malta.

The original convoy escorts meanwhile retired to the eastward and then proceeded to the north of Benghazi to try to intercept the enemy convoy but as it was bound for Tripoli they made no contact. They therefore retired eastwards and arrived at Alexandria during the night of 18/19 December. HMS Kipling had been damaged by a near miss on the 17th. One rating had been killed during this attack.
HMS Breconshire and her escorts arrived safely at Malta in the afternoon of December 18th. (29)

16 Dec 1941 HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) attacked a force of Italian warships off the Gulf of Taranto in position 39°33'N, 17°41'E. Four torpedoes were fired from long range but no hits were obtained.

The force attacked was most likely made up of the Italian heavy cruisers Gorizia and Trento escorted by the Italian destroyers Maestrale, Alfredo Oriani and Vincenzo Gioberti. They were scouting ten miles ahead of the Littorio battle group as the First Battle of Sirte was developing.
2210 B/16 - Heard H.E. bearing 335°.
2220 B/16 - In position 39°33'N, 17°41'E sighted destroyers and large units bearing 315°, range about 6 nautical miles, enemy course 140°, speed 20 knots. Started attack.
2234 B/16 - Fired four torpedoes at what appeared to be a cruiser at long range. The torpedoes missed and it is thought that the speed, estimated at 18 knots, may have been to little.
2235 B/16 - Dived.
2240 B/16 - Heard more HE of a number of destroyers with average resolutions of 220. Ships proceeding at 160 revs were also heard among these.
2305 B/16 - Increased depth to 120 feet.
2310 B/16 to 2313 B/16 - Three heavy units proceeded at 160 revs passed almost directly overhead. One of the propeller shafts of the third one made a clanking noise as it revolved.
2332 B/16 - Surfaced and commenced passing an enemy report to Malta W/T. The report was passed at 0010 B/17. (19)
17 Dec 1941 At 0158 B/17, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) received Capt. S.10's signal timed 0105 B/17 ordering her to return to Malta. (19)

19 Dec 1941 Around 0755 B/19, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) ended her 17th war patrol (15th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. This was the last patrol of her first commission. She was now to return to the U.K. to refit. (19)

3 Jan 1942 Around 1835 B/3, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) departed Malta for Gibraltar. She was escorted out until 2200 B/3 by the minesweeper HMS Abingdon (Lt. G.A. Simmers, RNR). Due to the shortage of torpedoes in Malta, she carried only two Mark II torpedoes.
5 Jan 1942 At 0315 B/5, south-south-east of Marettimo, in position 37°47'N, 12°09'E, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) sighted a southbound destroyer bearing 090° at a range of 4 miles.

There were no destroyers in this area at this day on this time. Perhaps the ship sighted was the naval auxiliary Mazara (984 GRT, built 1934) which sailed from Trapani at 0055 B/5 for Pantelleria. (5)

10 Jan 1942 At 1915 A/10, north-west of Oran, in position 36°15'N, 01°14'W, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) sighted a lighted merchant vessel 200° at a range of 6 miles proceeding on course 320°. (5)
12 Jan 1942 Around 1100 A/12, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) arrived at Gibraltar from Malta. (5)
17 Jan 1942 Around 1140 A/17, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) departed Gibraltar for Holy Loch.
She was to proceed though the following positions; (A) 36°00'N, 06°00'W at 1800 A/17. (B) 36°28'N, 07°05'W at 0300 A/18. (C) 36°40'N, 09°20'W at 1900 A/18. (D) 38°05'N, 11°05'W at 1200 A/19.

24 Jan 1942 In a signal timed 1217 A/24, Capt. S.3 ordered HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) that after passing through position 49°30'N, 10°00'E she was to proceed though position 270° - Bishops Rock - 5 miles to make rendezvous with HMS Rosemary (Lt.Cdr. J. Irwin, RNR) at 0845 A/6 in position 250° - Trevose Head - 10 miles for onward passage to Holy Loch. (30)
25 Jan 1942 At 2110 A/25, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) sighted Bishop Rock Lighthouse bearing 098° - 10 miles.

One minute later the signal timed 1904 A/25 from the Flag Officer Submarines was received which ordered her to take up a patrol position south-west of Brest, France in position 47°25'N, 06°30'W. The passage from Gibraltar to Holy Loch now became her 18th war patrol. Only cruisers and above were to be attacked.

In a signal timed 1926 A/25, the Flag Officer Submarines ordered HMS Utmost that owing to mines she was not proceed east of a line joining positions 48°00'N. 06°30'W, 47°12'N, 05°50'W and 46°45'N, 05°00'W. (5)
26 Jan 1942 At 2330 A/26, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) arrived in position 47°25'N, 06°30'W and commenced to patrol in the vicinity of that position. (5)

27 Jan 1942 In a signal timed 1534 A/27, the Flag Officer Submarines ordered HMS Utmost not to proceed inside an area bounded by a line joining positions 47°30'N, 05°55'W, 47°30'N, 05°30'W, 47°08'N, 04°45'W, 46°48'N, 05°04'W, 47°14'N, 05° 55'W owing to mines. Cancel my signal timed 1926 A/25. (30)

31 Jan 1942 At 2310 A/31, in position 47°30'N, 06°33'W, a U-boat was sighted bearing 040°, range 3 nautical miles. HMS Utmost dived but could not regain contact though the noise of an echo sounding machine was detected to the southward moving slowly westwards 40 minutes later.

This may have been the German U-boat U-753 on passage to St. Nazaire after her 1st war patrol in the North Atlantic. She was thus not westbound but eastbound. Also the Germans reported nothing. The reported position of U-753 at 0000 B/31 was however at least 30 miles from the position of HMS Utmost. No Allied submarines were in the area so the submarine can only have been enemy. (31)

2 Feb 1942 At 0100 A/2, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) left her patrol area and resumed her passage to Holy Loch in accordance with the Flag Officer Submarines signal timed 1304 A/30. She was to proceed to the rendezvous position through position 48°10'N, 06°20'W in accordance with Capt. S.3's signals timed 1031 A/31. In a signal timed 1214 A/1 the Capt. S.3 gave permission to proceed to Falmouth if fuel was required but this was not the case. (5)
3 Feb 1942 At 0800 A/3, HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) made rendezvous off Wolf Rock with her escort to Holy Loch, the armed yacht HMS Breda (Capt.(Retd.) A. E. Johnston, RN). (5)

Convoy WS 21S and the concentration of the escort forces

Convoy WS 21S departed the Clyde on 2 August 1942. The convoy was made up of the following ships; American freighters; Almeria Lykes (7773 GRT, built 1940), Santa Elisa (8379 GRT, built 1941), British freighters; Brisbane Star (12791 GRT, built 1937), Clan Ferguson (7347 GRT, built 1938), Deucalion (7516 GRT, built 1930), Dorset (10624 GRT, built 1934), Empire Hope (12688 GRT, built 1941), Glenorchy (8982 GRT, built 1939), Melbourne Star (11076 GRT, built 1936), Port Chalmers (8535 GRT, built 1933), Rochester Castle (7795 GRT, built 1937), Waimarama (12843 GRT, built 1938), Wairangi (12436 GRT, built 1935), and the American tanker; Ohio (9264 GRT, built 1940).

These ships were escorted by light cruisers HMS Nigeria (Capt. S.H. Paton, RN, flying the flag of the Rear-Admiral 10th C.S., H.M. Burrough, CB, DSO, RN), HMS Kenya (Capt. A.S. Russell, RN) and the destroyers HMS Wishart (Cdr. H.G. Scott, RN), HMS Venomous (Cdr. H.W. Falcon-Stewart, RN), HMS Wolverine (Lt.Cdr. P.W. Gretton, OBE, DSC, RN), HMS Malcolm (A/Cdr. A.B. Russell, RN), HMS Amazon (Lt.Cdr.(Emgy) Lord Teynham, RN), HMS Derwent (Cdr. R.H. Wright, DSC, RN) and HMS Zetland (Lt. J.V. Wilkinson, RN).

A cover force made up of departed Scapa Flow on the same day. This force was made up of the battleships HMS Nelson (Capt. H.B. Jacomb, RN, flying the flag of Vice-Admiral E.N. Syfret, CB, RN) and HMS Rodney (Capt. J.W. Rivett-Carnac, DSC, RN). They were escorted by the destroyers HMS Ashanti (Cdr. R.G. Onslow, DSO, RN), HMS Eskimo (Cdr. E.G. Le Geyt, RN), HMS Somali (Cdr. E.N.V. Currey, DSC, RN), HMS Tartar (Cdr. St.J.R.J. Tyrwhitt, DSC, RN), HMS Pathfinder (Cdr. E.A. Gibbs, DSO and Bar, RN), HMS Penn (Lt.Cdr. J.H. Swain, RN) and HMS Quentin (Lt.Cdr. A.H.P.
