This convoy was made up of the Italian tanker Saturno (5029 GRT, built 1914) and the Italian transports Beppe (4859 GRT, built 1912), Capo Orso (3149 GRT, built 1916) and Titania (5397 GRT, built 1918). They were escorted by the Italian destroyers Antonio da Noli, Giovanni da Verrazzano, Antonio Pigafetta, Ascari, Vincenzo Gioberti, Alfredo Oriani and the Italian torpedo boat Sagittario. The convoy came from Naples and Cagliari and was en-route to Tripoli.
0715 A/19 - Sighted a large camouflaged trawler bearing 170° at a range of 5 miles. Enemy course was 350°. The trawler looked very similar to the British 'Northern-class'.
0840 A/19 - In position 36°03'N, 11°56'E sighted two aircraft and the masts of four ships bearing 350°.

0850 A/19 - Identified the ships as three medium seized merchant ships in two columns escorted by seven destroyers disposed on each bow, beam, quarter and one right astern. Started attack to attack the leading merchant vessel of the port column.
0932 A/19 - Sighted a tanker, the rear ship of the Starboard column. Shifted target to this ship.
1003 A/19 - Fired the last two torpedoes aboard at this tanker. Range was 6000 yards. No hits were obtained. No counter attack followed. Utmost later (1056 A/19) surfaced to make an enemy report. (5)

19 Oct 1942 In a signal timed 0210 A/19, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information regarding the convoy to HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN), HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN), HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN), HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) and HMS P 211 (Cdr. B. Bryant, DSC, RN); Convoy in position 37°24'N, 11°53'E, course 180°, speed 8 knots at 0058 A/19.

In a signal timed 1130 A/19, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information regarding the convoy to the above mentioned submarines; My 0210 A/19. Convoy, composition unknown, course 180° in position 310° - Lampedusa - 35 miles. (47)
21 Oct 1942 At 1400 A/21, HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) ended her 23th war patrol (20th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
HMS Utmost, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) and HMS P 211 (Cdr. B. Bryant, DSC, RN) were swept in by HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN) which had joined around 1030 A/21. (5)

3 Nov 1942 Around 1410 A/3, HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) departed Malta for her 24th war patrol (21st in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off 'the toe of Italy'. She was to proceed through positions 35° 12'N, 15°10'E, and 36°00'N, 16°00'E en-route to her patrol area.
She, and HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) were swept out until 1620 A/3 by HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN). (5)
3 Nov 1942 For the daily and attack positions of HMS Utmost during her 24th war patrol see the map below.

6 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 1916 A/6, Capt. S.10 informed HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) that air reconnaissance had shown an enemy submarine position 36°20'N, 18°00'E at 1752 A/6 on course 310° at 10 knots. Utmost was ordered to patrol in position 37°53'N, 15°44'E by 0700 A/7.
This may have referred to the Italian submarine Ascianghi which was on passage to Augusta from a supply mission to Tobruk. (48)
7 Nov 1942 HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) attacked the Italian submarine Otaria with four torpedoes near Capo dell Armi. No hits were obtained.

Otaria (C.C. G. Caito) was proceeding from Naples to Taranto. One torpedo was observed and avoiding action taken immediately, it passed astern or just under her stern. Four explosion were heard which were probably torpedoes at the end of their run.
1215 A/7 - In position 37°52'N, 15°47'E sighted a submarine bearing 300°, range 5000 yards, coming round Capo del Armi.
1220 A/7 - The submarine steadied on a course of 135°. Started attack.
1226 A/7 - Identified the target as an Italian Liuzzi-class boat.
1241 A/7 - Fired four torpedoes from 400 yards. No hits were obtained. The torpedoes are thought to have run under due to the short range.
1243 A/7 - The submarine made of at high speed.
1252 A/7 - Heard the torpedoes explode on the bottom of the sea at the end of their run. The enemy was seen to dive. (5)

7 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 1943 A/7, Capt. S.10 informed HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) that air reconnaissance had shown an enemy submarine position 36°12'N, 18°14'E at 1706 A/7 on course 310° at 11 knots.
This may have referred to the Italian submarine Granito which was on passage to Augusta from a supply mission to Tobruk. (48)
8 Nov 1942 At 1209 A/8, HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) sighted smoke bearing 080° and Utmost turned towards to investigate.
At 1239 A/8, in position 37°52'N, 15°50'E, HMS Utmost started an attack on what appeared to be a small 2000-ton merchant vessel bearing 090° at a range of 11000 yards. Enemy course was 270°.
At 1300 A/8, the target was seen to be camouflaged with two masts and a long thin funnel. A gun was mounted on the forecastle and three HA guns were seen on the bridge structure.

At 1305 A/8, A/S transmissions were heard from the target. Although very tempted to fire at these ships - only four torpedoes remained for the expected bigger targets. The attack was therefore broken off. This A/S vessel patrolled all afternoon between Cape Spartivento and Cape del Armi assisted by one Ghibli aircraft.
At 2210 A/8, in position 37°46'N, 16°00'E, a lighted ship was seen coming towards from the direction of Messina. Closed to investigate.
At 2315 A/8, the ship was identified as being the Italian hospital ship Gradisca (13868 GRT, built 1913).
This was indeed the Gradisca which had sailed from Naples at 0230 A/8 and arrived at Tobruk at 0800 A/11.
At 2330 A/9, sighted masthead lights of a second ship approaching from the direction of Messina. Closed to investigate.
At 0030 A/8, this second ship was identified as being the Italian hospital ship Virgilio (11718 GRT, built 1928).
This was indeed the Virgilio which had sailed from Naples at 0830 A/8 and arrived at Benghazi at 0810 A/10. (5)
9 Nov 1942 At 0545 A/9, HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) heard diesel HE bearing 280° passing down the port side. Turned towards and came to periscope depth.

Two minutes later a motor torpedo boat was sighted steering 090° at a range of 3000 yards. Also an A/S trawler was sighted stopped, right astern and 1000 yards away. HMS Utmost went to 80 feet and took avoiding action.
At 0550 A/9, the trawler was heard pinging and hunting round.
At 0615 A/9, A/S transmissions and HE ceased and were not heard again. The trawler remained in sight all the forenoon. She was assisted by two aircraft. (5)

9 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 2307 A/9, Capt. S.10 informed HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) of the following; At 1750 A/9 air reconnaissance shows three cruisers and six destroyers in position 36°56'N, 21°11'E, course 273°, speed 23 knots. Move with all despatch to the following positions; Una to 080° - Cape Santa Croce - 5 miles and Utmost 100° - Cape Santa Croce - 1/4 mile. [Cape Santa Crose is off Augusta.]
In a signal timed 0025 A/10, these positions were amended to the following positions; Una 112° - Cape Santa Croce - 7 miles and Utmost 117° - Cape Santa Croce - 13 miles. (48)
10 Nov 1942 HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) attacked an enemy cruiser force with four torpedoes about 15 nautical miles east of Augusta, Sicily, Italy. No hits were obtained.

The enemy force was made up of the light cruisers Giuseppe Garibaldi, Luigi di Savoia Duca Degli Abruzzi and Emanuele Filiberto Duca d'Aosta. They were escorted by the destroyers Alpino, Bersagliere, Camicia Nera and Granatiere. This force had departed Navarino at 1630 A/8-1819 A/8 and arrived at Augusta at 0700 A/10-0830 A/10. The target was Emanuele Filiberto Duca d'Aosta which was missed by three torpedoes.
0611 A/10 - Sighted three cruisers and three destroyers. The rear cruiser was nearest to Utmost so this one was selected as the target.
0637 A/10 - In position 37°16'N, 15°31'E fired four torpedoes at the rear cruiser from 7000 yards. Went deep upon firing.
0646 A/10 - Heard a heavy explosion thought to be a torpedo hit. One minute later another explosion was heard.
0710 A/10 - Returned to periscope depth. Nothing in sight. Set course to clear the area and make an enemy report.
1220 A/10 - Surfaced and made an enemy report. (Utmost's 1201 A/10). Dived five minutes later.
1820 A/10 - Surfaced and left patrol to return to Malta as all torpedoes had been expended. (5)

10 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 0025 A/10, the Capt. S.10 sent the following signal to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN); My signal timed 2307 A/9. Amend submarine positions to read as follows. HMS Una 112° - Cape Santa Croce - 7 miles and HMS Utmost 117° - Cape Santa Croce - 13 miles.

In a signal timed 1154 A/10, the Capt. S.10 sent the following signal to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN); My signal timed 2307 A/9. The three cruisers are now in Augusta. Return to patrol positions given in the operation order of Operation Torch (Submarines) at economical speed. (48)

12 Nov 1942 Around 1215 A/12, HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) ended her 24th war patrol (21st in the Mediterranean) at Malta. She was swept in by HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN) which had joined around 1010 A/12. (5)

17 Nov 1942 HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) departed Malta for her 25th war patrol (22nd in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol between Tunisia and Sicily near Bizerta. During operation Torch only Africa-bound transports were to be attacked.
She was escorted out by HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN).
She was to pass through position 37°00'N, 15°27'E at 0300 A/l8 and then proceed to pass through Q.B.B. 255, diving at dawn to pass it at daylight on the 18th.

In a signal timed 2211 A/17, HMS Utmost informed Capt. S.10 that she had been unable to make the passage as far as had been anticipated apparently due to heavy air patrols. It was expected she would make the westbound passage through the enemy minefields off south-west Sicily (Q.B.B. 255) submerged at 80 feet during daylight on the 19th. (2)

20 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 1109 A/20, Capt. S.10 informed HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) that HMS P 221 (Lt. M.F.R. Ainslie, DSC, RN) reports traffic appearing to approach from westward hugging Cape Blanc. E-boats encountered at night, much air activity including S/M search. (49)

21 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 1541 A/21 Capt. S.10 informed HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) of the following; With air and light surface night patrols and moonlight condition it will be prudent to withdraw to seaward for charging in order to maintain offensive. Recommend following route. Una from position 37°10, 10°35 course 012°. Utmost from Cape Guardia 360° or from Canirock 060°. (49)

21 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 2320 A/21, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN), HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) and HMS P 247 (Lt. M.G.R. Lumby, DSC, RN) of the following; Convoy report consider correct interpretation, five destroyers, six merchant ships course 165° speed 4 [probably a mistake in the signal 10 is more likely] knots. Probably split and enter both ports [Bizerta and Tunis]. Objects to attack are the merchant ships. Aircraft will drop flares hourly. HMS Una intercept south of line Plane Island - Zembra Island, HMS P 247 north of this line.
This signal must have referred to a convoy mentioned in the following air reconnaissance reports; Position of ten unknown units 38°45'N, 09°20'E. (A/C Call Sign W5U duty 'U', 2110/21 to A.H.Q. Malta)
One cruiser, four destroyers, six transports in position 38°37'N, 09°25'E, course 180° at 10 knots. (A/C W5UU, 2200 A/21 to A.H.Q. Malta.)
Aircraft are to attack immediately, A.H.Q. Malta's signal timed 2215 A/21 to aircraft; W5U duty 'X', W5U duty 'C' and W5U duty 'S'.
Position of the enemy, 38°32'N, 09°32'E, course 180°, speed 10 knots. (A/C W5UX, 2235 A/21 to A.H.Q. Malta.)
One cruiser, four destroyers, four motor torpedo boats and five transports in position 38°15'N, 09°25'E. (A/C W5UU, 2349 A/21 to to A.H.Q. Malta.)

There is no convoy in this area which fits the discription of the air reconnaissance report. The only southbound convoy in this area at that period is the one made up of the transport Caterina Costa (Italian, 8060 GRT, built 1942) escorted by the destroyers Vincenzo Gioberti and Corsaro. They had sailed from Naples at 0620 A/21 and arrived at Bizerte at 0750 A/22. The two destroyers sailed from Bizerte at 0820 A/22 and arrived at Cagliari at 1623 A/22 to pick up elements of the San Marco Regiment (Italian Marines) and sailed again at 0100 A/23, arriving at Bizerte at 0830 A/24.

All available destroyers and torpedo boat files have been checked. There was no deployment of five destroyers in this area at that time, not even three! We have not been able to find any evidence of such a convoy at all.
The schooner Triglia left Trapani at 0900 A/21 via Porto Empedocle and arriveed at Tunis at 2000 A/23. The schooner Esmeralda left Trapani at 0145 A/21 and arrived at Tunis at 0830 A/22. (49)

22 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 0155 A/22, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN), HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) and HMS P 247 (Lt. M.G.R. Lumby, DSC, RN) were informed by Capt. S.10 of the following; Speed of convoy 10, mean course to midnight 180°. Consider land fall may be Cape Angela about 0500 hours. Still probably some ships destination Tunis. (49)

23 Nov 1942 HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) attacked the Italian auxiliary minelayer Barletta (1975 GRT) with (at least 3) torpedoes 3 miles north of Cape Blanc (near Bizerta). No hits were obtained.

Barletta was on passage from Palermo to Bizerta escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Groppo (C.C. Beniamine Farina). At 1215 A/23, Barletta observed three torpedo tracks, took evading action and dropped three depth charges. Groppo, who was ahead did not observe or hear anything. She was equipped with the German S-Gerät [sonar]. Barletta went on to Bizerta while the torpedo boat turned back to search for the submarine. Groppo observed only dead fish floating on the surface which marked the position of Barletta’s depth charges. She did not obtain a contact on the attacker.

HMS Utmost had meanwhile retired from the area and reported in her a signal timed 1231 A/23 that she had made a successful attack. Perhaps the depth charges from Barletta had been mistaken for hits. HMS Utmost also reported she was out of torpedoes, was proceeding direct to position 'B', would make the submerged passage of Q.B.B. 255 (enemy mined area to the south-west of Sicily) submerged during daylight hours on the 24th and that she expected to arrive at the end of the Malta searched channel around 1015 A/25. This signal was timed (T.O.O.) 1231 A/23 but was apparently only passed shortly after 2200 A/23 and received at Malta at 2247 A/23. Possibly it had not been possible to pass the signal earlier or HMS Utmost was unsuccessful in doing so, most likely to heavy enemy A/S activity in the area between Sicily and northern Tunisia.

It seems likely that during the attack on the Barletta, HMS Utmost expended her last four torpedoes since she reported she was out of torpedoes in this signal. It is not known on which target(s) she had expended her first salvo of four torpedoes though we think it may have been on the convoy [the Caterina Costa convoy, see 21 November 1942] that had been reported proceeding towards Bizerta / Tunis during the night of 21/22 November and the possible attack on this convoy was not noticed by the Italians.
The Italians had D/F'd HMS Utmost's signal and estimated it to be from a position about 010° - 35 miles from Bizerta (approximately 37°50'N, 10°00'E).

Capt. S.10 was convinced that when HMS Utmost had passed this signal she was on the direct route to position 'B. It was feared that when HMS Utmost failed to show up at Malta she was sunk by Italian patrol vessels which had sighted her during the night in the unfavourable moon conditions. Rome Radio claimed at noon on the 24th that the Italians had sunk an Allied submarine. (49)
25 Nov 1942 Evaluating enemy A/S activity after the war the Admiralty considered that the following Italian A/S attack might have encountered for HMS Utmost.

This is odd as Rome Radio claimed at noon on the 24th that an Allied submarine had been sunk by Italian A/S forces. The A/S attack the British considered it possible to have accounted for HMS Utmost is however only the 25th. Also according to her own signal HMS Utmost expected to return to Malta in the morning of the 25th having left patrol after having expended all torpedoes.

Around 0145 A/25, a convoy made up of the Italian transports XXI Aprile (4787 GRT, built 1919), Carlo Zeno (1446 GRT, built 1906) and Etruria (2633 GRT, built 1933) as well as the Italian motorised lighters MZ 705 and MZ 756. They were escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Sirio and Groppo.

The same day at 1155 A/25, an escorting aircraft dropped a bomb on the port side at a distance of 4000 metres and signalled a submarine. As the convoy turned to starboard, Groppo was detached to hunt the supposed enemy submarine. At 1210 A/25 an echo was obtained and four minutes later depth charges were dropped (the number is not given in the report, position 38°31.5'N, 12°01'E). The submarine was believed hit and the torpedo boat returned for a second run and claimed it sunk. After lingering in the scene for about an hour, Groppo left to rejoin the convoy.
It makes no sense for HMS Utmost, which had reported to have been out of torpedoes and returning to Malta to have proceeded this far north.

At 1353 A/25, the escorting Cant Z 506 dropped a bomb and signalled a submarine (38°32'N, 11°43'E). Again Groppo left the convoy to search the submarine. Seven minutes later the aircraft dropped a second bomb. At 1413.5 hours, Groppo sighted what appeared to be a torpedo track and at 1525 A/25 obtained an echo, she dropped 15 depth charges and lost the contact. However the result appeared inconclusive and Sirio ordered her to rejoin the convoy.
Italian analysis and assessment of Groppo's claims were not favourable; Capitano di Corvetta Farina was criticised for being too optimistic and the result of both attacks deemed to be doubtful.
It must be emphasized that echo contact and torpedo tracks were not necessarily indication that a submarine was present. A fair number of reported attacks by Axis and Allied forces were bogus.
ADM numbers indicate documents at the British National Archives at Kew, London.
